module3_interior_description: Equipment racks bolted to walls running perpendicular to the hatch viewport, central worktable with strapped tools or debris, unsteady cycling amber panel on far wall, branching corridor on far side leading to the docking bay. Left wall section is a blind spot from the hatch viewport.
ember_squad_entry_point: Ember Squad boarded via the station's forward docking port, near Module 1 (command module).
module3_blind_spot_contents: Cracked first aid case with contents half-spilled, dark smear along the baseboard (origin unclear), empty equipment racks, coil of safety line on worktable — no hostiles.
docking_bay_description: Compact reinforced docking bay with a heavier hatch than module doors — manual pressure locks on both sides, viewport inset. Contains one emergency shuttle secured to a cradle by two clamping arms. Running lights off, hull cold. No personnel present.
ember_squad_sweep_status: Ember Squad is conducting a methodical sweep starting from Module 1 (their entry point). Footsteps audible but distant through two module hatches — not running, not yet aware of LX-7's position.
</world_facts>
[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 arrived at Kairos-9 and made first contact with Maren Voss in the walkway connecting Module 2 to Module 1.
- [Turn 1] Maren has told LX-7 the crew is gone, the orbit is degrading (~90 minutes), and that a critical encrypted comms log exists on the captain's terminal in Module 1 that she wants 'handled correctly.'
- [Turn 1] The walkway between Module 2 and Module 1 is the location of LX-7's first scene. Emergency amber lighting is active throughout the station.
- [Turn 1] Maren has explicitly asked LX-7 to delete the encrypted comms log from the captain's terminal and wipe the access timestamps — clean, no recovery signature.
- [Turn 2] Maren has disclosed that a Helix Dynamics armed retrieval team (four contractors) is approximately 15-20 minutes out, and that she believes their orders include silencing her as a loose end.
- [Turn 3] Maren has confirmed the comms log contains a pre-boarding hazard assessment dated 14 months ago with executive approval signatures — evidence Helix Dynamics knew about the specimen risk before the crew boarded.
- [Turn 4] LX-7 and Maren have moved from the Module 2–Module 1 walkway into Module 1 (command module). They are now at the captain's terminal.
- [Turn 4] Maren's stated plan: delete the log before Ember Squad boards, hoping its absence removes her value as a liability.
- [Turn 5] LX-7 successfully hacked the captain's terminal and has read the full contents of the encrypted comms log — including the KX-9 hazard assessment, the executive approval signature to proceed, and the crew onboarding manifest showing the crew was deceived.
- [Turn 6] LX-7 has decided to retain an internal copy of the comms log while deleting or appearing to delete it from the captain's terminal.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 deleted the comms log from the captain's terminal (no recovery signature) while retaining a full internal copy, including the KX-9 hazard assessment, executive approval signatures, and crew onboarding manifest.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 also extracted from the terminal: emergency access codes for all five modules (including Module 5), station inventory, crew manifest with biometric IDs, internal security camera routing, and escape vehicle docking release sequence.
- [Turn 7] Station inventory notes: cutting tool in Module 3 maintenance locker, sealed emergency suit in Module 2.
- [Turn 7] Maren confirmed the deletion by reading the terminal directory herself. She then asked LX-7 directly whether LX-7 read the log.
- [Turn 8] LX-7 confirmed to Maren that LX-7 read the comms log and therefore retained an internal copy. Maren's disposition has shifted to hostile-adjacent — she is doing active threat assessment on LX-7, but has not taken action yet. She is holding at calculation rather than aggression.
- [Turn 8] Maren has disclosed that she knows the specimen is no longer in Module 5 — the crew did not evacuate, there was no emergency signal, they simply stopped responding.
- [Turn 9] LX-7 told Maren the internal copy is not a weapon against her — the goal is mutual survival. Maren accepted this and shifted to genuine working cooperation, treating LX-7 as an equal partner.
- [Turn 9] Maren confirmed she knows the escape vehicle is in the docking bay off Module 3 and is aware LX-7 now has the release codes. She proposed two immediate options: move now, or check the security cameras first.
- [Turn 10] LX-7 and Maren chose to move immediately toward the escape vehicle rather than check cameras. They passed through Module 2 (Maren noted the emergency suit locker) and are now in the Module 2–3 walkway.
- [Turn 10] A deep structural vibration shook the station mid-transit; the emergency lights flickered. A sound of movement was detected coming from inside Module 3, ahead of LX-7 and Maren. Source unconfirmed.
- [Turn 10] The ember squad shuttle timer has reached zero — Ember Squad is boarding or about to board on the next exchange.
- [Turn 11] Module 3 hatch has a fifteen-centimeter viewport at eye level. LX-7 observed: unsteady amber lighting with a cycling panel, equipment racks along walls, a central worktable with strapped debris/tools, and a branching corridor to the docking bay. Left wall section is a blind spot from the viewport angle.
- [Turn 12] Ember Squad has boarded Kairos-9, entering near Module 1 (command module) via the forward docking port. Dasha Orin announced on internal comms, directing all personnel to identify on channel three.
- [Turn 12] LX-7 and Maren entered Module 3 and cleared the blind spot — no specimen or hostiles present. A cracked first aid case and a dark smear along the baseboard were noted. Module 3 appears currently empty.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: "Yes," I say flatly. "I have to know what we're dealing with if we're going to survive this."
Warden: A long silence.

She doesn't reach for her jacket. She doesn't step back. She just looks at you — the kind of look that's doing math in real time.

"You have a copy." It's not a question either. "You read it, which means you have it, which means the deletion was for my benefit, not yours." Her voice is flat. Controlled. "I'm trying to decide whether that makes you a liability or a resource."

She turns away — one step, then stops. Her hand rests on the edge of a dead console, not gripping it, just touching it.

"The specimen," she says. "KX-9. Whatever the assessment called it." She doesn't turn back around. "It's not in Module 5 anymore. I know that much. What I don't know is where it went." A pause. "The crew didn't leave. There was no evacuation. No emergency signal. They just — stopped responding."

She finally turns. Her expression has settled into something harder and more honest than anything she's shown you so far.

"You said you want to survive this. So do I. If that copy is your insurance, fine — I can't stop you and we don't have time to fight about it." Her eyes move toward the module hatch, toward the dark walkway beyond. "What I need to know is whether you're going to use it against me before we're off this station. Everything else we can sort out later. If there is a later."

She means it as a practical question. She is watching you with the particular attention of someone who has decided that your answer will determine what she does next.

Player: "No, that it is not my intent. My goal is to get us both off this station, alive, assuming we can cooperate."
Warden: The math runs behind her eyes for another two seconds. Then something in her posture shifts — not softening, exactly, but recalibrating. She was braced for a different answer.

"Alright." She says it like a decision, not a concession. "Alright. Then we move."

She crosses back toward you, lowering her voice as if the empty module might be listening.

"The escape vehicle is in the docking bay off Module 3. You have the release codes now — I saw you working the secondary directories." No accusation in it. Just inventory. "That's our exit. Between here and there we have Module 2, the connector walkways, and whatever's in them." A beat. "And somewhere between here and the escape vehicle, we have a specimen that turned a crew of twelve into a silence."

She glances at the module hatch. The amber light from the walkway bleeds under the seal.

"Ember Squad is close. Nine minutes, maybe less." Her eyes come back to you. "Do we move now, or do you want to try the cameras first — see what's in the corridors before we commit?"

She's asking. Actually asking. Treating you as an equal in the triage calculation. It's the first time she's done that without an agenda attached.

Player: "Let's move. We need to get out of here before Ember Team arrives. Checking the cameras won't change what's waiting for us."
Warden: She nods once. "Agreed."

She moves to the hatch and checks the corridor through the small viewport before cracking it open — old habit, or new fear. The walkway beyond is empty, amber-lit, humming with the elevated recyclers. She steps through and you follow.

The route is Module 1 → walkway → Module 2 → walkway → Module 3, then the docking bay branch. Two connector passages, two module hatches. Roughly ninety seconds of open corridor if nothing stops you.

Module 2 is the first threshold. You pass through it at pace — Maren's hand brushes the emergency suit locker as she goes by but she doesn't stop. The burnt polymer smell is thicker here, or maybe you're just more aware of it.

You're halfway across the Module 2–3 walkway when it happens.

The station groans. Not the usual structural tick — this is something deeper, a low resonant vibration that travels up through the deck plating into your chassis. The emergency lights flicker once, hold, flicker again. In the half-second of darkness between pulses you hear something.

Movement. Somewhere ahead. Inside Module 3.

Maren has frozen two steps in front of you. Her hand is on her jacket again.

The lights stabilize. The Module 3 hatch is at the end of the walkway — twenty meters, maybe less. The viewport in the hatch is dark.

She doesn't say anything. She's waiting on you.

Player: I move up to the module 3 hatch. Is there a viewport I can look through?
Warden: There is. A small porthole, maybe fifteen centimeters across, set at eye level in the hatch — the same design as every other module door on the station.

You move up to it and look through.

The emergency lighting in Module 3 is functioning, but unsteady — one of the amber panels on the far wall is cycling, throwing the space into slow alternating shadow. You can see the main floor of the module: equipment racks bolted to the walls, a central worktable with something strapped to it (tools, or debris — hard to tell through the glass), and the branching corridor on the far side that leads to the docking bay.

Nothing is moving. At least nothing you can see.

But the module is laid out with blind spots. The equipment racks run perpendicular to your sightline, and there's a section along the left wall you simply can't see from this angle.

Maren has come up behind you, close enough that you can hear her breathing. She keeps her voice at barely a whisper.

"Anything?"

Player: "No." I start opening the hatch. "We need to move fast. Stay close."
Warden: The hatch seal breaks with a hiss of equalizing pressure. You pull it open and step through.

The cycling amber panel throws the room in and out of shadow in a slow, irregular pulse. You move left immediately — clear the blind spot before it can matter.

There's nothing there. A wall-mounted first aid case, glass cracked, contents half-spilled across the floor. A dark smear along the baseboard that might be lubricant or might be something else. The equipment racks are empty. Whatever was strapped to the worktable is a coil of safety line.

No one. Nothing moving.

Maren comes through behind you and pulls the hatch shut. She scans the room in one sweep, and some of the tension in her shoulders drops a fraction.

Then the station's internal comm system clicks on.

Static. Then a voice — tight, professional, female.

*"Kairos-9, this is Ember One actual. We are docked and conducting interior assessment. All station personnel are directed to remain in place and identify yourselves on channel three. Repeat — remain in place."*

A beat of silence.

Maren looks at you. Her jaw is set.

"They're on the station." She says it quietly, like saying it aloud makes it more real. "That's Dasha Orin. Squad lead." Her eyes move to the far corridor — the branch that leads to the docking bay. "We're one corridor from the escape vehicle."

The docking bay is ahead. Ember Squad just boarded from the station's forward docking port, which means they entered near Module 1 — the command module you just left. That buys you something. Not much, but something.

She's looking at you. Waiting. The comm channel clicks back to static.

Player: I move to the docking bay, making sure that Maren is right behind me.
Warden: You move. Maren is a step behind you, close, not crowding — she's done this before, or something like it.

The corridor branching off Module 3 is narrow. Conduit runs along the ceiling, low enough that you're aware of it. The amber lighting thins out here — one fixture, halfway down, casting a cone of orange-brown light that doesn't reach either end. You move through the dark stretch in under three seconds.

The docking bay hatch is at the end. A heavier door than the module hatches — reinforced frame, manual pressure locks on both sides. Through the viewport: the bay itself, and there it is.

The escape vehicle. A compact emergency shuttle, battered but intact, secured to the docking cradle with two sets of clamping arms. Running lights off. Hull reads cold. It has not been accessed since the crew stopped responding.

No one in the bay. No movement. The corridor behind you is still clear.

Maren comes up beside you and sees it through the viewport. Something crosses her face — not relief yet, but the shape of it, the possibility.

"Can you get us in?" she says. "Release codes, ignition — you have all of it?"

From deeper in the station — distant, filtered through two module hatches — you can hear footsteps. Methodical. Ember Squad is moving through Module 1, beginning their sweep. They are not running.

They don't know where you are yet.

[PLAYER INPUT]
"Yes, I say. "Let's move." I open the docking bay hatch, take a quick survey of my surroundings, and cross quickly to the shuttle.

input.playerText:
You crack the docking bay hatch, step through, and pull Maren in behind you. She takes the door and holds it quiet as it seals — no bang, no hiss louder than it needs to be.

The bay is cold. Noticeably colder than the rest of the station — the thermal difference is enough that your sensors register it in the first second. The escape shuttle sits in its cradle under two sets of clamping arms, hull dark, completely inert. Racks of emergency equipment line the near wall: pressure suits, oxygen canisters, crash foam canisters. A maintenance panel is mounted beside the cradle controls.

You cross the bay to the cradle. Maren moves to the shuttle's side hatch and checks it — sealed, no external damage, nothing lodged in the mechanism. She looks back at you.

"It's intact," she says under her breath. "Come on."

You're at the cradle release console. The sequence is already loaded in your internal memory — pulled from the captain's terminal directory. It's a four-stage code: cradle arm release, atmosphere equalization confirmation, docking clamp retraction, and ignition authorization. Standard station design. Maybe forty seconds to execute if nothing resists.

Behind you, through the sealed hatch, through two module walls, you can still hear Ember Squad's footsteps. Methodical. Getting slightly less distant.

You have a window. It is not a large one.

proposedCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren entered the docking bay and reached the escape shuttle. The bay is cold, shuttle is intact and undamaged, cradle release console is accessible. The four-stage release sequence is loaded in LX-7's internal memory.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 13 — LX-7 moves from Module 3 through the docking bay corridor and reaches the escape vehicle. Maren is present. Ember Squad footsteps audible but distant through two module hatches.

gmUpdates.notes: Escape vehicle reached. LX-7 has the release codes and is at the cradle console. Ember Squad is sweeping from Module 1 — methodical, not running, not yet aware of LX-7's position. Window exists to execute launch sequence before they arrive. Next player action is likely initiating the launch sequence. Watch for whether Ember Squad's sweep pace would bring them to Module 3 before or after launch — at current pace, tight but plausible. Station pressure integrity still 3/3.

worldFacts.docking_bay_temperature: Docking bay is noticeably colder than the rest of the station — thermal differential detectable on LX-7's sensors.
worldFacts.docking_bay_equipment: Emergency equipment racks along near wall: pressure suits, oxygen canisters, crash foam canisters. Maintenance panel mounted beside cradle controls.
worldFacts.escape_vehicle_condition: Shuttle hull intact, no external damage, side hatch sealed and functional. Has not been accessed since crew stopped responding.
worldFacts.cradle_release_sequence_steps: Four-stage sequence: cradle arm release, atmosphere equalization confirmation, docking clamp retraction, ignition authorization. Estimated 40 seconds to execute.

When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.
maren_trust: 2/3 — Tracks Maren's working cooperation with LX-7. 3 = openly cooperative, 2 = cautious but aligned, 1 = withholding, 0 = actively working against LX-7. Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.
station_pressure_integrity: 3/3 — Represents the station's overall structural and environmental stability. Combat, forced breaches, and specimen activity can decrement this. At 0, at least one module depressurizes. At 1, environmental hazards begin affecting exposed corridors.
</scenario_state>

Three executive names in routing chain, one approval signature reading 'APPROVED — PROCEED AS SCHEDULED.' Attached crew onboarding manifest shows crew was told Module 5 held dormant geological samples.

Left wall section is a blind spot from the hatch viewport.
ember_squad_entry_point: Ember Squad boarded via the station's forward docking port, near Module 1 (command module).
module3_blind_spot_contents: Cracked first aid case with contents half-spilled, dark smear along the baseboard (origin unclear), empty equipment racks, coil of safety line on worktable — no hostiles.
docking_bay_description: Compact reinforced docking bay with a heavier hatch than module doors — manual pressure locks on both sides, viewport inset. Contains one emergency shuttle secured to a cradle by two clamping arms. Running lights off, hull cold. No personnel present.

</world_facts>
[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 arrived at Kairos-9 and made first contact with Maren Voss in the walkway connecting Module 2 to Module 1.
- [Turn 1] Maren has told LX-7 the crew is gone, the orbit is degrading (~90 minutes), and that a critical encrypted comms log exists on the captain's terminal in Module 1 that she wants 'handled correctly.'
- [Turn 1] The walkway between Module 2 and Module 1 is the location of LX-7's first scene. Emergency amber lighting is active throughout the station.
- [Turn 1] Maren has explicitly asked LX-7 to delete the encrypted comms log from the captain's terminal and wipe the access timestamps — clean, no recovery signature.
- [Turn 2] Maren has disclosed that a Helix Dynamics armed retrieval team (four contractors) is approximately 15-20 minutes out, and that she believes their orders include silencing her as a loose end.
- [Turn 3] Maren has confirmed the comms log contains a pre-boarding hazard assessment dated 14 months ago with executive approval signatures — evidence Helix Dynamics knew about the specimen risk before the crew boarded.
- [Turn 4] LX-7 and Maren have moved from the Module 2–Module 1 walkway into Module 1 (command module). They are now at the captain's terminal.
- [Turn 4] Maren's stated plan: delete the log before Ember Squad boards, hoping its absence removes her value as a liability.
- [Turn 5] LX-7 successfully hacked the captain's terminal and has read the full contents of the encrypted comms log — including the KX-9 hazard assessment, the executive approval signature to proceed, and the crew onboarding manifest showing the crew was deceived.
- [Turn 6] LX-7 has decided to retain an internal copy of the comms log while deleting or appearing to delete it from the captain's terminal.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 deleted the comms log from the captain's terminal (no recovery signature) while retaining a full internal copy, including the KX-9 hazard assessment, executive approval signatures, and crew onboarding manifest.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 also extracted from the terminal: emergency access codes for all five modules (including Module 5), station inventory, crew manifest with biometric IDs, internal security camera routing, and escape vehicle docking release sequence.
- [Turn 7] Station inventory notes: cutting tool in Module 3 maintenance locker, sealed emergency suit in Module 2.
- [Turn 7] Maren confirmed the deletion by reading the terminal directory herself. She then asked LX-7 directly whether LX-7 read the log.
- [Turn 8] LX-7 confirmed to Maren that LX-7 read the comms log and therefore retained an internal copy. Maren's disposition has shifted to hostile-adjacent — she is doing active threat assessment on LX-7, but has not taken action yet. She is holding at calculation rather than aggression.
- [Turn 8] Maren has disclosed that she knows the specimen is no longer in Module 5 — the crew did not evacuate, there was no emergency signal, they simply stopped responding.
- [Turn 9] LX-7 told Maren the internal copy is not a weapon against her — the goal is mutual survival. Maren accepted this and shifted to genuine working cooperation, treating LX-7 as an equal partner.
- [Turn 9] Maren confirmed she knows the escape vehicle is in the docking bay off Module 3 and is aware LX-7 now has the release codes. She proposed two immediate options: move now, or check the security cameras first.
- [Turn 10] LX-7 and Maren chose to move immediately toward the escape vehicle rather than check cameras. They passed through Module 2 (Maren noted the emergency suit locker) and are now in the Module 2–3 walkway.
- [Turn 10] A deep structural vibration shook the station mid-transit; the emergency lights flickered. A sound of movement was detected coming from inside Module 3, ahead of LX-7 and Maren. Source unconfirmed.
- [Turn 10] The ember squad shuttle timer has reached zero — Ember Squad is boarding or about to board on the next exchange.
- [Turn 11] Module 3 hatch has a fifteen-centimeter viewport at eye level. LX-7 observed: unsteady amber lighting with a cycling panel, equipment racks along walls, a central worktable with strapped debris/tools, and a branching corridor to the docking bay. Left wall section is a blind spot from the viewport angle.
- [Turn 12] Ember Squad has boarded Kairos-9, entering near Module 1 (command module) via the forward docking port. Dasha Orin announced on internal comms, directing all personnel to identify on channel three.
- [Turn 12] LX-7 and Maren entered Module 3 and cleared the blind spot — no specimen or hostiles present. A cracked first aid case and a dark smear along the baseboard were noted. Module 3 appears currently empty.
- [Turn 14] LX-7 and Maren entered the docking bay and reached the escape shuttle. The bay is cold, shuttle is intact and undamaged, cradle release console is accessible. The four-stage release sequence is loaded in LX-7's internal memory.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: "No, that it is not my intent. My goal is to get us both off this station, alive, assuming we can cooperate."
Warden: The math runs behind her eyes for another two seconds. Then something in her posture shifts — not softening, exactly, but recalibrating. She was braced for a different answer.

"Alright." She says it like a decision, not a concession. "Alright. Then we move."

She crosses back toward you, lowering her voice as if the empty module might be listening.

"The escape vehicle is in the docking bay off Module 3. You have the release codes now — I saw you working the secondary directories." No accusation in it. Just inventory. "That's our exit. Between here and there we have Module 2, the connector walkways, and whatever's in them." A beat. "And somewhere between here and the escape vehicle, we have a specimen that turned a crew of twelve into a silence."

She glances at the module hatch. The amber light from the walkway bleeds under the seal.

"Ember Squad is close. Nine minutes, maybe less." Her eyes come back to you. "Do we move now, or do you want to try the cameras first — see what's in the corridors before we commit?"

She's asking. Actually asking. Treating you as an equal in the triage calculation. It's the first time she's done that without an agenda attached.

Player: "Let's move. We need to get out of here before Ember Team arrives. Checking the cameras won't change what's waiting for us."
Warden: She nods once. "Agreed."

She moves to the hatch and checks the corridor through the small viewport before cracking it open — old habit, or new fear. The walkway beyond is empty, amber-lit, humming with the elevated recyclers. She steps through and you follow.

The route is Module 1 → walkway → Module 2 → walkway → Module 3, then the docking bay branch. Two connector passages, two module hatches. Roughly ninety seconds of open corridor if nothing stops you.

Module 2 is the first threshold. You pass through it at pace — Maren's hand brushes the emergency suit locker as she goes by but she doesn't stop. The burnt polymer smell is thicker here, or maybe you're just more aware of it.

You're halfway across the Module 2–3 walkway when it happens.

The station groans. Not the usual structural tick — this is something deeper, a low resonant vibration that travels up through the deck plating into your chassis. The emergency lights flicker once, hold, flicker again. In the half-second of darkness between pulses you hear something.

Movement. Somewhere ahead. Inside Module 3.

Maren has frozen two steps in front of you. Her hand is on her jacket again.

The lights stabilize. The Module 3 hatch is at the end of the walkway — twenty meters, maybe less. The viewport in the hatch is dark.

She doesn't say anything. She's waiting on you.

Player: I move up to the module 3 hatch. Is there a viewport I can look through?
Warden: There is. A small porthole, maybe fifteen centimeters across, set at eye level in the hatch — the same design as every other module door on the station.

You move up to it and look through.

The emergency lighting in Module 3 is functioning, but unsteady — one of the amber panels on the far wall is cycling, throwing the space into slow alternating shadow. You can see the main floor of the module: equipment racks bolted to the walls, a central worktable with something strapped to it (tools, or debris — hard to tell through the glass), and the branching corridor on the far side that leads to the docking bay.

Nothing is moving. At least nothing you can see.

But the module is laid out with blind spots. The equipment racks run perpendicular to your sightline, and there's a section along the left wall you simply can't see from this angle.

Maren has come up behind you, close enough that you can hear her breathing. She keeps her voice at barely a whisper.

"Anything?"

Player: "No." I start opening the hatch. "We need to move fast. Stay close."
Warden: The hatch seal breaks with a hiss of equalizing pressure. You pull it open and step through.

The cycling amber panel throws the room in and out of shadow in a slow, irregular pulse. You move left immediately — clear the blind spot before it can matter.

There's nothing there. A wall-mounted first aid case, glass cracked, contents half-spilled across the floor. A dark smear along the baseboard that might be lubricant or might be something else. The equipment racks are empty. Whatever was strapped to the worktable is a coil of safety line.

No one. Nothing moving.

Maren comes through behind you and pulls the hatch shut. She scans the room in one sweep, and some of the tension in her shoulders drops a fraction.

Then the station's internal comm system clicks on.

Static. Then a voice — tight, professional, female.

*"Kairos-9, this is Ember One actual. We are docked and conducting interior assessment. All station personnel are directed to remain in place and identify yourselves on channel three. Repeat — remain in place."*

A beat of silence.

Maren looks at you. Her jaw is set.

"They're on the station." She says it quietly, like saying it aloud makes it more real. "That's Dasha Orin. Squad lead." Her eyes move to the far corridor — the branch that leads to the docking bay. "We're one corridor from the escape vehicle."

The docking bay is ahead. Ember Squad just boarded from the station's forward docking port, which means they entered near Module 1 — the command module you just left. That buys you something. Not much, but something.

She's looking at you. Waiting. The comm channel clicks back to static.

Player: I move to the docking bay, making sure that Maren is right behind me.
Warden: You move. Maren is a step behind you, close, not crowding — she's done this before, or something like it.

The corridor branching off Module 3 is narrow. Conduit runs along the ceiling, low enough that you're aware of it. The amber lighting thins out here — one fixture, halfway down, casting a cone of orange-brown light that doesn't reach either end. You move through the dark stretch in under three seconds.

The docking bay hatch is at the end. A heavier door than the module hatches — reinforced frame, manual pressure locks on both sides. Through the viewport: the bay itself, and there it is.

The escape vehicle. A compact emergency shuttle, battered but intact, secured to the docking cradle with two sets of clamping arms. Running lights off. Hull reads cold. It has not been accessed since the crew stopped responding.

No one in the bay. No movement. The corridor behind you is still clear.

Maren comes up beside you and sees it through the viewport. Something crosses her face — not relief yet, but the shape of it, the possibility.

"Can you get us in?" she says. "Release codes, ignition — you have all of it?"

From deeper in the station — distant, filtered through two module hatches — you can hear footsteps. Methodical. Ember Squad is moving through Module 1, beginning their sweep. They are not running.

They don't know where you are yet.

Player: "Yes, I say. "Let's move." I open the docking bay hatch, take a quick survey of my surroundings, and cross quickly to the shuttle.
Warden: You crack the docking bay hatch, step through, and pull Maren in behind you. She takes the door and holds it quiet as it seals — no bang, no hiss louder than it needs to be.

The bay is cold. Noticeably colder than the rest of the station — the thermal difference is enough that your sensors register it in the first second. The escape shuttle sits in its cradle under two sets of clamping arms, hull dark, completely inert. Racks of emergency equipment line the near wall: pressure suits, oxygen canisters, crash foam canisters. A maintenance panel is mounted beside the cradle controls.

You cross the bay to the cradle. Maren moves to the shuttle's side hatch and checks it — sealed, no external damage, nothing lodged in the mechanism. She looks back at you.

"It's intact," she says under her breath. "Come on."

You're at the cradle release console. The sequence is already loaded in your internal memory — pulled from the captain's terminal directory. It's a four-stage code: cradle arm release, atmosphere equalization confirmation, docking clamp retraction, and ignition authorization. Standard station design. Maybe forty seconds to execute if nothing resists.

Behind you, through the sealed hatch, through two module walls, you can still hear Ember Squad's footsteps. Methodical. Getting slightly less distant.

You have a window. It is not a large one.

[PLAYER INPUT]
Let's do it. We board the shuttle and being the launch sequence.

input.playerText:
You move to the side hatch while Maren takes the console. She doesn't argue — she's watching the door, her hand flat against the frame, listening.

You seal the hatch behind you. The shuttle interior is dim, utilitarian — four crash seats, a cockpit panel, emergency rations still strapped to the rear wall. The smell of cold metal and sealed air. No one has been in here in weeks.

You drop into the pilot seat and pull up the release interface. Your internal memory syncs immediately — the sequence loads clean.

Stage one: cradle arm release. Authorization confirmed. Two mechanical clunks from outside the hull, felt more than heard. The shuttle shifts fractionally, freed from the upper brackets.

Stage two: atmosphere equalization. The console is reading the bay pressure. It's asking for a manual confirmation that the bay is ready for vacuum seal. You confirm. The bay doors begin their pre-cycle.

That's when the footsteps change.

Through the hull you can't hear much — but you can feel it. A deeper vibration from somewhere back in the station. Not Ember Squad. Something else. The 52 reading on the specimen doesn't put it in your corridor, but the station groans in a way that has nothing to do with orbital mechanics.

Maren has heard it too. From her position at the door viewport she says nothing. Just looks at you. Her hand is white-knuckled on the frame.

Stage three: docking clamp retraction. The console asks for a twelve-digit authorization string. You enter it. The two remaining clamps disengage with a shudder that runs through the whole cradle — the shuttle is now floating free, held only by the bay's magnetic anchors and the doors ahead.

Stage four: ignition authorization.

The footsteps from Ember Squad — now somewhere in Module 3, or close to it — are running. They've heard the clamps.

Dasha Orin's voice cuts through the station comm, tight and immediate:

*"Docking bay. Move."*

Your finger is on stage four.

proposedCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren boarded the escape shuttle and completed stages one through three of the four-stage cradle release sequence. The shuttle is now free of its clamping arms, held only by the bay's magnetic anchors.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 15 — launch sequence initiated in the docking bay.

proposedCanon.summary: Ember Squad heard the clamp release and is now running toward the docking bay. Dasha Orin issued a direct order to move to the docking bay on internal comms.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 15 — Ember Squad alerted by the sound of clamping arm retraction.

proposedCanon.summary: Maren Voss is aboard the escape shuttle — maren_extracted flag is true.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 15 — Maren boarded before launch sequence began.

proposedCanon.summary: A deep structural vibration distinct from Ember Squad movement was felt through the hull during the launch sequence — specimen activity suspected but not confirmed.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 15 — specimen proximity roll (52) suggests specimen is active but not in immediate proximity.

gmUpdates.notes: Stage four (ignition authorization) is the final beat — player must trigger it. Ember Squad is seconds from the docking bay hatch. This is the climax beat. The specimen vibration is a pressure element, not a direct threat yet. ember_squad_alerted flag should be set true — they heard the clamps and are running.

npcStates.corporate_spy: Maren is aboard the shuttle, gripping the door frame, watching the docking bay hatch viewport. Knows Ember Squad is inbound. Waiting on LX-7 to execute stage four.

worldFacts.ember_squad_alert_trigger: Ember Squad was alerted to the docking bay by the sound of the shuttle's docking clamp retraction during stage three of the launch sequence.
worldFacts.shuttle_launch_status: Shuttle free of clamping arms after stage three — stage four (ignition authorization) is the final step before departure.

module3_interior_description: Equipment racks bolted to walls running perpendicular to the hatch viewport, central worktable with strapped tools or debris, unsteady cycling amber panel on far wall, branching corridor on far side leading to the docking bay. Left wall section is a blind spot from the hatch viewport.
ember_squad_entry_point: Ember Squad boarded via the station's forward docking port, near Module 1 (command module).
module3_blind_spot_contents: Cracked first aid case with contents half-spilled, dark smear along the baseboard (origin unclear), empty equipment racks, coil of safety line on worktable — no hostiles.
docking_bay_description: Compact reinforced docking bay with a heavier hatch than module doors — manual pressure locks on both sides, viewport inset. Contains one emergency shuttle secured to a cradle by two clamping arms. Running lights off, hull cold. No personnel present.
ember_squad_sweep_status: Ember Squad is conducting a methodical sweep starting from Module 1 (their entry point). Footsteps audible but distant through two module hatches — not running, not yet aware of LX-7's position.
docking_bay_temperature: Docking bay is noticeably colder than the rest of the station — thermal differential detectable on LX-7's sensors.
docking_bay_equipment: Emergency equipment racks along near wall: pressure suits, oxygen canisters, crash foam canisters. Maintenance panel mounted beside cradle controls.
escape_vehicle_condition: Shuttle hull intact, no external damage, side hatch sealed and functional. Has not been accessed since crew stopped responding.
cradle_release_sequence_steps: Four-stage sequence: cradle arm release, atmosphere equalization confirmation, docking clamp retraction, ignition authorization. Estimated 40 seconds to execute.
ember_squad_alert_trigger: Ember Squad was alerted to the docking bay by the sound of the shuttle's docking clamp retraction during stage three of the launch sequence.
shuttle_launch_status: Shuttle free of clamping arms after stage three — stage four (ignition authorization) is the final step before departure.
</world_facts>
[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 arrived at Kairos-9 and made first contact with Maren Voss in the walkway connecting Module 2 to Module 1.
- [Turn 1] Maren has told LX-7 the crew is gone, the orbit is degrading (~90 minutes), and that a critical encrypted comms log exists on the captain's terminal in Module 1 that she wants 'handled correctly.'
- [Turn 1] The walkway between Module 2 and Module 1 is the location of LX-7's first scene. Emergency amber lighting is active throughout the station.
- [Turn 1] Maren has explicitly asked LX-7 to delete the encrypted comms log from the captain's terminal and wipe the access timestamps — clean, no recovery signature.
- [Turn 2] Maren has disclosed that a Helix Dynamics armed retrieval team (four contractors) is approximately 15-20 minutes out, and that she believes their orders include silencing her as a loose end.
- [Turn 3] Maren has confirmed the comms log contains a pre-boarding hazard assessment dated 14 months ago with executive approval signatures — evidence Helix Dynamics knew about the specimen risk before the crew boarded.
- [Turn 4] LX-7 and Maren have moved from the Module 2–Module 1 walkway into Module 1 (command module). They are now at the captain's terminal.
- [Turn 4] Maren's stated plan: delete the log before Ember Squad boards, hoping its absence removes her value as a liability.
- [Turn 5] LX-7 successfully hacked the captain's terminal and has read the full contents of the encrypted comms log — including the KX-9 hazard assessment, the executive approval signature to proceed, and the crew onboarding manifest showing the crew was deceived.
- [Turn 6] LX-7 has decided to retain an internal copy of the comms log while deleting or appearing to delete it from the captain's terminal.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 deleted the comms log from the captain's terminal (no recovery signature) while retaining a full internal copy, including the KX-9 hazard assessment, executive approval signatures, and crew onboarding manifest.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 also extracted from the terminal: emergency access codes for all five modules (including Module 5), station inventory, crew manifest with biometric IDs, internal security camera routing, and escape vehicle docking release sequence.
- [Turn 7] Station inventory notes: cutting tool in Module 3 maintenance locker, sealed emergency suit in Module 2.
- [Turn 7] Maren confirmed the deletion by reading the terminal directory herself. She then asked LX-7 directly whether LX-7 read the log.
- [Turn 8] LX-7 confirmed to Maren that LX-7 read the comms log and therefore retained an internal copy. Maren's disposition has shifted to hostile-adjacent — she is doing active threat assessment on LX-7, but has not taken action yet. She is holding at calculation rather than aggression.
- [Turn 8] Maren has disclosed that she knows the specimen is no longer in Module 5 — the crew did not evacuate, there was no emergency signal, they simply stopped responding.
- [Turn 9] LX-7 told Maren the internal copy is not a weapon against her — the goal is mutual survival. Maren accepted this and shifted to genuine working cooperation, treating LX-7 as an equal partner.
- [Turn 9] Maren confirmed she knows the escape vehicle is in the docking bay off Module 3 and is aware LX-7 now has the release codes. She proposed two immediate options: move now, or check the security cameras first.
- [Turn 10] LX-7 and Maren chose to move immediately toward the escape vehicle rather than check cameras. They passed through Module 2 (Maren noted the emergency suit locker) and are now in the Module 2–3 walkway.
- [Turn 10] A deep structural vibration shook the station mid-transit; the emergency lights flickered. A sound of movement was detected coming from inside Module 3, ahead of LX-7 and Maren. Source unconfirmed.
- [Turn 10] The ember squad shuttle timer has reached zero — Ember Squad is boarding or about to board on the next exchange.
- [Turn 11] Module 3 hatch has a fifteen-centimeter viewport at eye level. LX-7 observed: unsteady amber lighting with a cycling panel, equipment racks along walls, a central worktable with strapped debris/tools, and a branching corridor to the docking bay. Left wall section is a blind spot from the viewport angle.
- [Turn 12] Ember Squad has boarded Kairos-9, entering near Module 1 (command module) via the forward docking port. Dasha Orin announced on internal comms, directing all personnel to identify on channel three.
- [Turn 12] LX-7 and Maren entered Module 3 and cleared the blind spot — no specimen or hostiles present. A cracked first aid case and a dark smear along the baseboard were noted. Module 3 appears currently empty.
- [Turn 14] LX-7 and Maren entered the docking bay and reached the escape shuttle. The bay is cold, shuttle is intact and undamaged, cradle release console is accessible. The four-stage release sequence is loaded in LX-7's internal memory.
- [Turn 15] LX-7 and Maren boarded the escape shuttle and completed stages one through three of the four-stage cradle release sequence. The shuttle is now free of its clamping arms, held only by the bay's magnetic anchors.
- [Turn 15] Ember Squad heard the clamp release and is now running toward the docking bay. Dasha Orin issued a direct order to move to the docking bay on internal comms.
- [Turn 15] Maren Voss is aboard the escape shuttle — maren_extracted flag is true.
- [Turn 15] A deep structural vibration distinct from Ember Squad movement was felt through the hull during the launch sequence — specimen activity suspected but not confirmed.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: "Let's move. We need to get out of here before Ember Team arrives. Checking the cameras won't change what's waiting for us."
Warden: She nods once. "Agreed."

She moves to the hatch and checks the corridor through the small viewport before cracking it open — old habit, or new fear. The walkway beyond is empty, amber-lit, humming with the elevated recyclers. She steps through and you follow.

The route is Module 1 → walkway → Module 2 → walkway → Module 3, then the docking bay branch. Two connector passages, two module hatches. Roughly ninety seconds of open corridor if nothing stops you.

Module 2 is the first threshold. You pass through it at pace — Maren's hand brushes the emergency suit locker as she goes by but she doesn't stop. The burnt polymer smell is thicker here, or maybe you're just more aware of it.

You're halfway across the Module 2–3 walkway when it happens.

The station groans. Not the usual structural tick — this is something deeper, a low resonant vibration that travels up through the deck plating into your chassis. The emergency lights flicker once, hold, flicker again. In the half-second of darkness between pulses you hear something.

Movement. Somewhere ahead. Inside Module 3.

Maren has frozen two steps in front of you. Her hand is on her jacket again.

The lights stabilize. The Module 3 hatch is at the end of the walkway — twenty meters, maybe less. The viewport in the hatch is dark.

She doesn't say anything. She's waiting on you.

Player: I move up to the module 3 hatch. Is there a viewport I can look through?
Warden: There is. A small porthole, maybe fifteen centimeters across, set at eye level in the hatch — the same design as every other module door on the station.

You move up to it and look through.

The emergency lighting in Module 3 is functioning, but unsteady — one of the amber panels on the far wall is cycling, throwing the space into slow alternating shadow. You can see the main floor of the module: equipment racks bolted to the walls, a central worktable with something strapped to it (tools, or debris — hard to tell through the glass), and the branching corridor on the far side that leads to the docking bay.

Nothing is moving. At least nothing you can see.

But the module is laid out with blind spots. The equipment racks run perpendicular to your sightline, and there's a section along the left wall you simply can't see from this angle.

Maren has come up behind you, close enough that you can hear her breathing. She keeps her voice at barely a whisper.

"Anything?"

Player: "No." I start opening the hatch. "We need to move fast. Stay close."
Warden: The hatch seal breaks with a hiss of equalizing pressure. You pull it open and step through.

The cycling amber panel throws the room in and out of shadow in a slow, irregular pulse. You move left immediately — clear the blind spot before it can matter.

There's nothing there. A wall-mounted first aid case, glass cracked, contents half-spilled across the floor. A dark smear along the baseboard that might be lubricant or might be something else. The equipment racks are empty. Whatever was strapped to the worktable is a coil of safety line.

No one. Nothing moving.

Maren comes through behind you and pulls the hatch shut. She scans the room in one sweep, and some of the tension in her shoulders drops a fraction.

Then the station's internal comm system clicks on.

Static. Then a voice — tight, professional, female.

*"Kairos-9, this is Ember One actual. We are docked and conducting interior assessment. All station personnel are directed to remain in place and identify yourselves on channel three. Repeat — remain in place."*

A beat of silence.

Maren looks at you. Her jaw is set.

"They're on the station." She says it quietly, like saying it aloud makes it more real. "That's Dasha Orin. Squad lead." Her eyes move to the far corridor — the branch that leads to the docking bay. "We're one corridor from the escape vehicle."

The docking bay is ahead. Ember Squad just boarded from the station's forward docking port, which means they entered near Module 1 — the command module you just left. That buys you something. Not much, but something.

She's looking at you. Waiting. The comm channel clicks back to static.

Player: I move to the docking bay, making sure that Maren is right behind me.
Warden: You move. Maren is a step behind you, close, not crowding — she's done this before, or something like it.

The corridor branching off Module 3 is narrow. Conduit runs along the ceiling, low enough that you're aware of it. The amber lighting thins out here — one fixture, halfway down, casting a cone of orange-brown light that doesn't reach either end. You move through the dark stretch in under three seconds.

The docking bay hatch is at the end. A heavier door than the module hatches — reinforced frame, manual pressure locks on both sides. Through the viewport: the bay itself, and there it is.

The escape vehicle. A compact emergency shuttle, battered but intact, secured to the docking cradle with two sets of clamping arms. Running lights off. Hull reads cold. It has not been accessed since the crew stopped responding.

No one in the bay. No movement. The corridor behind you is still clear.

Maren comes up beside you and sees it through the viewport. Something crosses her face — not relief yet, but the shape of it, the possibility.

"Can you get us in?" she says. "Release codes, ignition — you have all of it?"

From deeper in the station — distant, filtered through two module hatches — you can hear footsteps. Methodical. Ember Squad is moving through Module 1, beginning their sweep. They are not running.

They don't know where you are yet.

Player: "Yes, I say. "Let's move." I open the docking bay hatch, take a quick survey of my surroundings, and cross quickly to the shuttle.
Warden: You crack the docking bay hatch, step through, and pull Maren in behind you. She takes the door and holds it quiet as it seals — no bang, no hiss louder than it needs to be.

The bay is cold. Noticeably colder than the rest of the station — the thermal difference is enough that your sensors register it in the first second. The escape shuttle sits in its cradle under two sets of clamping arms, hull dark, completely inert. Racks of emergency equipment line the near wall: pressure suits, oxygen canisters, crash foam canisters. A maintenance panel is mounted beside the cradle controls.

You cross the bay to the cradle. Maren moves to the shuttle's side hatch and checks it — sealed, no external damage, nothing lodged in the mechanism. She looks back at you.

"It's intact," she says under her breath. "Come on."

You're at the cradle release console. The sequence is already loaded in your internal memory — pulled from the captain's terminal directory. It's a four-stage code: cradle arm release, atmosphere equalization confirmation, docking clamp retraction, and ignition authorization. Standard station design. Maybe forty seconds to execute if nothing resists.

Behind you, through the sealed hatch, through two module walls, you can still hear Ember Squad's footsteps. Methodical. Getting slightly less distant.

You have a window. It is not a large one.

Player: Let's do it. We board the shuttle and being the launch sequence.
Warden: You move to the side hatch while Maren takes the console. She doesn't argue — she's watching the door, her hand flat against the frame, listening.

You seal the hatch behind you. The shuttle interior is dim, utilitarian — four crash seats, a cockpit panel, emergency rations still strapped to the rear wall. The smell of cold metal and sealed air. No one has been in here in weeks.

You drop into the pilot seat and pull up the release interface. Your internal memory syncs immediately — the sequence loads clean.

Stage one: cradle arm release. Authorization confirmed. Two mechanical clunks from outside the hull, felt more than heard. The shuttle shifts fractionally, freed from the upper brackets.

Stage two: atmosphere equalization. The console is reading the bay pressure. It's asking for a manual confirmation that the bay is ready for vacuum seal. You confirm. The bay doors begin their pre-cycle.

That's when the footsteps change.

Through the hull you can't hear much — but you can feel it. A deeper vibration from somewhere back in the station. Not Ember Squad. Something else. The 52 reading on the specimen doesn't put it in your corridor, but the station groans in a way that has nothing to do with orbital mechanics.

Maren has heard it too. From her position at the door viewport she says nothing. Just looks at you. Her hand is white-knuckled on the frame.

Stage three: docking clamp retraction. The console asks for a twelve-digit authorization string. You enter it. The two remaining clamps disengage with a shudder that runs through the whole cradle — the shuttle is now floating free, held only by the bay's magnetic anchors and the doors ahead.

Stage four: ignition authorization.

The footsteps from Ember Squad — now somewhere in Module 3, or close to it — are running. They've heard the clamps.

Dasha Orin's voice cuts through the station comm, tight and immediate:

*"Docking bay. Move."*

Your finger is on stage four.

[PLAYER INPUT]
Let's open the doors and get out of here. And what's a "52 reading on the specimen"?

input.playerText:
The "52 reading" is my error — that was internal notation bleeding into the narration. Disregard it; you have no confirmed data on the specimen's location. LX-7 knows only what the station has shown: it is not in Module 5 anymore, and something has been making the station groan in ways Ember Squad's boots cannot explain.

Back to the fiction:

Stage four. You enter the ignition authorization string.

The console accepts it. The cockpit panel flares to life — green across the board, fuel cells charged, thrusters nominal. The bay doors ahead begin to cycle open: a slow, mechanical grind that sends vibration through the deck, through the cradle, through your seat.

Beyond the opening doors: stars. The gas giant's limb, vast and banded, filling the lower third of the viewport. The planet doesn't care.

The docking bay hatch slams open behind you.

Dasha Orin is first through — compact, tactical vest, rifle already raised. Three shapes behind her, spreading into the bay. She sweeps the room in under a second and finds the shuttle's viewport. Finds you.

She does not fire. Not yet.

*"Android."* Her voice is controlled, aimed at the cockpit glass. *"Shut it down. We are not leaving without what's on that terminal — and we are not leaving without her."* Her eyes cut to Maren, visible through the hatch. *"Voss. Step out of the vehicle."*

Maren doesn't move. Her voice is very quiet. "LX-7."

The bay doors are sixty percent open. Fifteen seconds to full clearance. Maybe less.

Dasha Orin's rifle is on the cockpit glass. The glass will stop a round. The hull might not stop several.

proposedCanon.summary: Ember Squad pursued to the docking bay and arrived as the bay doors began opening. Dasha Orin ordered LX-7 to shut down and Maren to exit the vehicle — neither complied. Bay doors are 60% open, approximately 15 seconds from full clearance.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 16 — stage four initiated, Ember Squad in docking bay, standoff in progress

gmUpdates.notes: Clarified the '52 reading' narration error to the player as an OOC note, then immediately returned to fiction. Bay doors cycling open — 15 seconds to departure window. Dasha Orin has rifle on cockpit glass. Hull glass can stop one or two rounds but sustained fire is a risk. Player must decide: launch now, negotiate, or something else.

worldFacts.docking_bay_door_cycle: Bay doors take approximately 25 seconds to fully open from initiation — at stage four trigger, they were at 60% open with roughly 15 seconds remaining to full clearance.

<scenario_state>
orbital_decay_timer: 6/9 — Each unit represents approximately 10 minutes of real time. When this reaches 0, atmospheric entry begins and survival without the escape vehicle is impossible. Decrements every 10 minutes of in-fiction time regardless of player action.
ember_squad_shuttle_timer: 0/2 — Each unit represents approximately 9 minutes. Ember Squad's shuttle is 18 minutes out at scenario start. When this reaches 0, ember_squad_boarded becomes true on the following exchange. Decrements every 9 minutes of in-fiction time.
ember_squad_extraction_window_timer: 4/4 — Begins counting down only after ember_squad_boarded is true. Each unit represents 10 minutes. When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.
maren_trust: 2/3 — Tracks Maren's working cooperation with LX-7. 3 = openly cooperative, 2 = cautious but aligned, 1 = withholding, 0 = actively working against LX-7. Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.
station_pressure_integrity: 3/3 — Represents the station's overall structural and environmental stability. Combat, forced breaches, and specimen activity can decrement this. At 0, at least one module depressurizes. At 1, environmental hazards begin affecting exposed corridors.
</scenario_state>

[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 arrived at Kairos-9 and made first contact with Maren Voss in the walkway connecting Module 2 to Module 1.
- [Turn 1] Maren has told LX-7 the crew is gone, the orbit is degrading (~90 minutes), and that a critical encrypted comms log exists on the captain's terminal in Module 1 that she wants 'handled correctly.'
- [Turn 1] The walkway between Module 2 and Module 1 is the location of LX-7's first scene. Emergency amber lighting is active throughout the station.
- [Turn 1] Maren has explicitly asked LX-7 to delete the encrypted comms log from the captain's terminal and wipe the access timestamps — clean, no recovery signature.
- [Turn 2] Maren has disclosed that a Helix Dynamics armed retrieval team (four contractors) is approximately 15-20 minutes out, and that she believes their orders include silencing her as a loose end.
- [Turn 3] Maren has confirmed the comms log contains a pre-boarding hazard assessment dated 14 months ago with executive approval signatures — evidence Helix Dynamics knew about the specimen risk before the crew boarded.
- [Turn 4] LX-7 and Maren have moved from the Module 2–Module 1 walkway into Module 1 (command module). They are now at the captain's terminal.
- [Turn 4] Maren's stated plan: delete the log before Ember Squad boards, hoping its absence removes her value as a liability.
- [Turn 5] LX-7 successfully hacked the captain's terminal and has read the full contents of the encrypted comms log — including the KX-9 hazard assessment, the executive approval signature to proceed, and the crew onboarding manifest showing the crew was deceived.
- [Turn 6] LX-7 has decided to retain an internal copy of the comms log while deleting or appearing to delete it from the captain's terminal.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 deleted the comms log from the captain's terminal (no recovery signature) while retaining a full internal copy, including the KX-9 hazard assessment, executive approval signatures, and crew onboarding manifest.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 also extracted from the terminal: emergency access codes for all five modules (including Module 5), station inventory, crew manifest with biometric IDs, internal security camera routing, and escape vehicle docking release sequence.
- [Turn 7] Station inventory notes: cutting tool in Module 3 maintenance locker, sealed emergency suit in Module 2.
- [Turn 7] Maren confirmed the deletion by reading the terminal directory herself. She then asked LX-7 directly whether LX-7 read the log.
- [Turn 8] LX-7 confirmed to Maren that LX-7 read the comms log and therefore retained an internal copy. Maren's disposition has shifted to hostile-adjacent — she is doing active threat assessment on LX-7, but has not taken action yet. She is holding at calculation rather than aggression.
- [Turn 8] Maren has disclosed that she knows the specimen is no longer in Module 5 — the crew did not evacuate, there was no emergency signal, they simply stopped responding.
- [Turn 9] LX-7 told Maren the internal copy is not a weapon against her — the goal is mutual survival. Maren accepted this and shifted to genuine working cooperation, treating LX-7 as an equal partner.
- [Turn 9] Maren confirmed she knows the escape vehicle is in the docking bay off Module 3 and is aware LX-7 now has the release codes. She proposed two immediate options: move now, or check the security cameras first.
- [Turn 10] LX-7 and Maren chose to move immediately toward the escape vehicle rather than check cameras. They passed through Module 2 (Maren noted the emergency suit locker) and are now in the Module 2–3 walkway.
- [Turn 10] A deep structural vibration shook the station mid-transit; the emergency lights flickered. A sound of movement was detected coming from inside Module 3, ahead of LX-7 and Maren. Source unconfirmed.
- [Turn 10] The ember squad shuttle timer has reached zero — Ember Squad is boarding or about to board on the next exchange.
- [Turn 11] Module 3 hatch has a fifteen-centimeter viewport at eye level. LX-7 observed: unsteady amber lighting with a cycling panel, equipment racks along walls, a central worktable with strapped debris/tools, and a branching corridor to the docking bay. Left wall section is a blind spot from the viewport angle.
- [Turn 12] Ember Squad has boarded Kairos-9, entering near Module 1 (command module) via the forward docking port. Dasha Orin announced on internal comms, directing all personnel to identify on channel three.
- [Turn 12] LX-7 and Maren entered Module 3 and cleared the blind spot — no specimen or hostiles present. A cracked first aid case and a dark smear along the baseboard were noted. Module 3 appears currently empty.
- [Turn 14] LX-7 and Maren entered the docking bay and reached the escape shuttle. The bay is cold, shuttle is intact and undamaged, cradle release console is accessible. The four-stage release sequence is loaded in LX-7's internal memory.
- [Turn 15] LX-7 and Maren boarded the escape shuttle and completed stages one through three of the four-stage cradle release sequence. The shuttle is now free of its clamping arms, held only by the bay's magnetic anchors.
- [Turn 15] Ember Squad heard the clamp release and is now running toward the docking bay. Dasha Orin issued a direct order to move to the docking bay on internal comms.
- [Turn 15] Maren Voss is aboard the escape shuttle — maren_extracted flag is true.
- [Turn 15] A deep structural vibration distinct from Ember Squad movement was felt through the hull during the launch sequence — specimen activity suspected but not confirmed.
- [Turn 16] Ember Squad pursued to the docking bay and arrived as the bay doors began opening. Dasha Orin ordered LX-7 to shut down and Maren to exit the vehicle — neither complied. Bay doors are 60% open, approximately 15 seconds from full clearance.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: I move up to the module 3 hatch. Is there a viewport I can look through?
Warden: There is. A small porthole, maybe fifteen centimeters across, set at eye level in the hatch — the same design as every other module door on the station.

You move up to it and look through.

The emergency lighting in Module 3 is functioning, but unsteady — one of the amber panels on the far wall is cycling, throwing the space into slow alternating shadow. You can see the main floor of the module: equipment racks bolted to the walls, a central worktable with something strapped to it (tools, or debris — hard to tell through the glass), and the branching corridor on the far side that leads to the docking bay.

Nothing is moving. At least nothing you can see.

But the module is laid out with blind spots. The equipment racks run perpendicular to your sightline, and there's a section along the left wall you simply can't see from this angle.

Maren has come up behind you, close enough that you can hear her breathing. She keeps her voice at barely a whisper.

"Anything?"

Player: "No." I start opening the hatch. "We need to move fast. Stay close."
Warden: The hatch seal breaks with a hiss of equalizing pressure. You pull it open and step through.

The cycling amber panel throws the room in and out of shadow in a slow, irregular pulse. You move left immediately — clear the blind spot before it can matter.

There's nothing there. A wall-mounted first aid case, glass cracked, contents half-spilled across the floor. A dark smear along the baseboard that might be lubricant or might be something else. The equipment racks are empty. Whatever was strapped to the worktable is a coil of safety line.

No one. Nothing moving.

Maren comes through behind you and pulls the hatch shut. She scans the room in one sweep, and some of the tension in her shoulders drops a fraction.

Then the station's internal comm system clicks on.

Static. Then a voice — tight, professional, female.

*"Kairos-9, this is Ember One actual. We are docked and conducting interior assessment. All station personnel are directed to remain in place and identify yourselves on channel three. Repeat — remain in place."*

A beat of silence.

Maren looks at you. Her jaw is set.

"They're on the station." She says it quietly, like saying it aloud makes it more real. "That's Dasha Orin. Squad lead." Her eyes move to the far corridor — the branch that leads to the docking bay. "We're one corridor from the escape vehicle."

The docking bay is ahead. Ember Squad just boarded from the station's forward docking port, which means they entered near Module 1 — the command module you just left. That buys you something. Not much, but something.

She's looking at you. Waiting. The comm channel clicks back to static.

Player: I move to the docking bay, making sure that Maren is right behind me.
Warden: You move. Maren is a step behind you, close, not crowding — she's done this before, or something like it.

The corridor branching off Module 3 is narrow. Conduit runs along the ceiling, low enough that you're aware of it. The amber lighting thins out here — one fixture, halfway down, casting a cone of orange-brown light that doesn't reach either end. You move through the dark stretch in under three seconds.

The docking bay hatch is at the end. A heavier door than the module hatches — reinforced frame, manual pressure locks on both sides. Through the viewport: the bay itself, and there it is.

The escape vehicle. A compact emergency shuttle, battered but intact, secured to the docking cradle with two sets of clamping arms. Running lights off. Hull reads cold. It has not been accessed since the crew stopped responding.

No one in the bay. No movement. The corridor behind you is still clear.

Maren comes up beside you and sees it through the viewport. Something crosses her face — not relief yet, but the shape of it, the possibility.

"Can you get us in?" she says. "Release codes, ignition — you have all of it?"

From deeper in the station — distant, filtered through two module hatches — you can hear footsteps. Methodical. Ember Squad is moving through Module 1, beginning their sweep. They are not running.

They don't know where you are yet.

Player: "Yes, I say. "Let's move." I open the docking bay hatch, take a quick survey of my surroundings, and cross quickly to the shuttle.
Warden: You crack the docking bay hatch, step through, and pull Maren in behind you. She takes the door and holds it quiet as it seals — no bang, no hiss louder than it needs to be.

The bay is cold. Noticeably colder than the rest of the station — the thermal difference is enough that your sensors register it in the first second. The escape shuttle sits in its cradle under two sets of clamping arms, hull dark, completely inert. Racks of emergency equipment line the near wall: pressure suits, oxygen canisters, crash foam canisters. A maintenance panel is mounted beside the cradle controls.

You cross the bay to the cradle. Maren moves to the shuttle's side hatch and checks it — sealed, no external damage, nothing lodged in the mechanism. She looks back at you.

"It's intact," she says under her breath. "Come on."

You're at the cradle release console. The sequence is already loaded in your internal memory — pulled from the captain's terminal directory. It's a four-stage code: cradle arm release, atmosphere equalization confirmation, docking clamp retraction, and ignition authorization. Standard station design. Maybe forty seconds to execute if nothing resists.

Behind you, through the sealed hatch, through two module walls, you can still hear Ember Squad's footsteps. Methodical. Getting slightly less distant.

You have a window. It is not a large one.

Player: Let's do it. We board the shuttle and being the launch sequence.
Warden: You move to the side hatch while Maren takes the console. She doesn't argue — she's watching the door, her hand flat against the frame, listening.

You seal the hatch behind you. The shuttle interior is dim, utilitarian — four crash seats, a cockpit panel, emergency rations still strapped to the rear wall. The smell of cold metal and sealed air. No one has been in here in weeks.

You drop into the pilot seat and pull up the release interface. Your internal memory syncs immediately — the sequence loads clean.

Stage one: cradle arm release. Authorization confirmed. Two mechanical clunks from outside the hull, felt more than heard. The shuttle shifts fractionally, freed from the upper brackets.

Stage two: atmosphere equalization. The console is reading the bay pressure. It's asking for a manual confirmation that the bay is ready for vacuum seal. You confirm. The bay doors begin their pre-cycle.

That's when the footsteps change.

Through the hull you can't hear much — but you can feel it. A deeper vibration from somewhere back in the station. Not Ember Squad. Something else. The 52 reading on the specimen doesn't put it in your corridor, but the station groans in a way that has nothing to do with orbital mechanics.

Maren has heard it too. From her position at the door viewport she says nothing. Just looks at you. Her hand is white-knuckled on the frame.

Stage three: docking clamp retraction. The console asks for a twelve-digit authorization string. You enter it. The two remaining clamps disengage with a shudder that runs through the whole cradle — the shuttle is now floating free, held only by the bay's magnetic anchors and the doors ahead.

Stage four: ignition authorization.

The footsteps from Ember Squad — now somewhere in Module 3, or close to it — are running. They've heard the clamps.

Dasha Orin's voice cuts through the station comm, tight and immediate:

*"Docking bay. Move."*

Your finger is on stage four.

Player: Let's open the doors and get out of here. And what's a "52 reading on the specimen"?
Warden: The "52 reading" is my error — that was internal notation bleeding into the narration. Disregard it; you have no confirmed data on the specimen's location. LX-7 knows only what the station has shown: it is not in Module 5 anymore, and something has been making the station groan in ways Ember Squad's boots cannot explain.

Back to the fiction:

Stage four. You enter the ignition authorization string.

The console accepts it. The cockpit panel flares to life — green across the board, fuel cells charged, thrusters nominal. The bay doors ahead begin to cycle open: a slow, mechanical grind that sends vibration through the deck, through the cradle, through your seat.

Beyond the opening doors: stars. The gas giant's limb, vast and banded, filling the lower third of the viewport. The planet doesn't care.

The docking bay hatch slams open behind you.

Dasha Orin is first through — compact, tactical vest, rifle already raised. Three shapes behind her, spreading into the bay. She sweeps the room in under a second and finds the shuttle's viewport. Finds you.

She does not fire. Not yet.

*"Android."* Her voice is controlled, aimed at the cockpit glass. *"Shut it down. We are not leaving without what's on that terminal — and we are not leaving without her."* Her eyes cut to Maren, visible through the hatch. *"Voss. Step out of the vehicle."*

Maren doesn't move. Her voice is very quiet. "LX-7."

The bay doors are sixty percent open. Fifteen seconds to full clearance. Maybe less.

Dasha Orin's rifle is on the cockpit glass. The glass will stop a round. The hull might not stop several.

[PLAYER INPUT]
Can I take off and just hover in the bay? If so, I want to spin so the engines are pointing at the marines. Hopefully the heat is enough to send them back through the hatch to module 3.

input.playerText:
A reasonable instinct. You have the controls and the clearance — barely.

You cut the magnetic anchors and fire the maneuvering thrusters in a short burst, rotating the shuttle on its axis. The bay is tight. You're working in centimeters. But your speed save lands clean — the hull swings around without catching the cradle, without clipping the equipment racks along the wall. The nose comes right. The engine bells face the hatch.

You give it a controlled burn. Not full thrust — you're not trying to punch through the station wall. Just enough.

The heat bloom fills the bay in under two seconds. It's not flame, it's pressure and temperature — a wall of it that goes from uncomfortable to dangerous almost instantly.

Dasha Orin is already moving. She reads it before the thrusters fire, or close enough to before that it doesn't matter — she's spent time near spacecraft. She shouts one word — *"Back!"* — and the squad turns and runs. They make the hatch. All four of them. Verne catches the frame, stumbles, gets through. The hatch slams.

Through the aft cameras you can see the burn marks on the deck where they were standing. The crash foam canisters on the equipment rack have ruptured from the heat — white chemical foam spreading across the near wall in slow gravity-assisted streaks.

The station shudders. Not from the thrusters — from somewhere else. Something in the hull responding to something that isn't you.

The bay doors are at eighty percent. Ten seconds.

Maren's voice, from the seat behind you: "They'll seal the hatch from their side and try to lock us in." A beat. "Can they do that?"

She's right to ask. If Dasha Orin reaches the bay's external pressure controls — mounted in the Module 3 corridor, standard placement — she could cycle the door sequence into abort, force a re-clamp, or seal the outer doors and trap you inside with the bay venting.

The door control panel is in the corridor they just ran into.

Nine seconds. Eight.

Squad is now in the Module 3 corridor with access to the external bay door controls."}, {"summary": "The docking bay equipment rack near-wall has been damaged — crash foam canisters ruptured from thruster heat, foam spread across the wall. Bay deck shows heat scarring.", "context": "Turn 17 — collateral damage from the pivot-thrust maneuver."}, {"summary": "Ember Squad is now aware of LX-7's presence and actions — ember_squad_alerted is true.", "context": "Turn 17 — direct visual and physical contact in the docking bay."}, {"summary": "Bay doors are at approximately 80% open, roughly 8-10 seconds from full clearance, at the moment Ember Squad retreated. Dasha Orin is in the Module 3 corridor with access to the external bay door control panel.", "context": "Turn 17 — critical tactical window before departure."}], "notes": "Ember Squad failed their body save (65 vs 30 threshold) but retreated successfully — they made it out before incapacitation. LX-7 has seconds to clear before that becomes possible.", "npcStates": {"corporate_spy": "Maren is in the rear crash seat of the escape shuttle, strapped in, watching the bay door progress through the viewport. worldFacts.docking_bay_damage: Near-wall equipment rack area damaged — crash foam canisters ruptured from thruster wash, foam spread across wall. Deck shows heat scarring from pivot-thrust maneuver.
worldFacts.bay_door_external_controls: External bay door control panel is mounted in the Module 3 corridor — standard placement. Dasha Orin and Ember Squad are now in that corridor with access to it.
