<scenario_state>
orbital_decay_timer: 5/9 — Each unit represents approximately 10 minutes of real time. When this reaches 0, atmospheric entry begins and survival without the escape vehicle is impossible. Decrements every 10 minutes of in-fiction time regardless of player action.
ember_squad_shuttle_timer: 0/2 — Each unit represents approximately 9 minutes. Ember Squad's shuttle is 18 minutes out at scenario start. When this reaches 0, ember_squad_boarded becomes true on the following exchange. Decrements every 9 minutes of in-fiction time.
ember_squad_extraction_window_timer: 3/4 — Begins counting down only after ember_squad_boarded is true. Each unit represents 10 minutes. When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.
maren_trust: 2/3 — Tracks Maren's working cooperation with LX-7. 3 = openly cooperative, 2 = cautious but aligned, 1 = withholding, 0 = actively working against LX-7. Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.
station_pressure_integrity: 3/3 — Represents the station's overall structural and environmental stability. Combat, forced breaches, and specimen activity can decrement this. At 0, at least one module depressurizes. At 1, environmental hazards begin affecting exposed corridors.
</scenario_state>

<world_facts>
station_lighting: Emergency amber and red lighting throughout all accessible modules and walkways — standard overhead lighting is offline.
maren_first_contact_location: Walkway connecting Module 2 to Module 1, near the Module 1 entrance.
orbital_decay_window: Approximately 90 minutes from scenario start before atmospheric entry becomes unsurvivable.
air_quality: Air recyclers running at elevated capacity; air has a faint burnt polymer smell throughout accessible corridors.
module1_description: Command module: central workstation ringed by dead secondary screens, personal effects still at stations — magneted coffee bulb, jacket draped over a chair. Emergency amber lighting only.
captains_terminal_location: Far end of Module 1 (command module), central workstation position.
comms_log_contents: KX-9 hazard assessment: specimen exhibits autonomous behavior outside projected parameters, containment confidence insufficient. Three executive names in routing chain, one approval signature reading 'APPROVED — PROCEED AS SCHEDULED.' Attached crew onboarding manifest shows crew was told Module 5 held dormant geological samples.
module3_maintenance_locker: Contains a cutting tool, per station inventory records.
module2_emergency_suit: A sealed emergency suit is stored in Module 2, per station inventory records.
escape_vehicle_release_sequence: Docking bay release sequence for the escape vehicle is now stored in LX-7's internal memory.
module5_access_codes: Emergency access codes for all five modules, including Module 5's fused panel, are now stored in LX-7's internal memory.
ember_squad_current_eta: Ember Squad shuttle timer has reached zero — boarding imminent.
module2_to_module3_walkway_length: Approximately twenty meters from Module 2 hatch to Module 3 hatch.
module3_hatch_viewport: Small porthole, approximately fifteen centimeters across, set at eye level in the Module 3 hatch — standard design matching other module doors.
module3_interior_description: Equipment racks bolted to walls running perpendicular to the hatch viewport, central worktable with strapped tools or debris, unsteady cycling amber panel on far wall, branching corridor on far side leading to the docking bay. Left wall section is a blind spot from the hatch viewport.
ember_squad_entry_point: Ember Squad boarded via the station's forward docking port, near Module 1 (command module).
module3_blind_spot_contents: Cracked first aid case with contents half-spilled, dark smear along the baseboard (origin unclear), empty equipment racks, coil of safety line on worktable — no hostiles.
docking_bay_description: Compact reinforced docking bay with a heavier hatch than module doors — manual pressure locks on both sides, viewport inset. Contains one emergency shuttle secured to a cradle by two clamping arms. Running lights off, hull cold. No personnel present.
ember_squad_sweep_status: Ember Squad is conducting a methodical sweep starting from Module 1 (their entry point). Footsteps audible but distant through two module hatches — not running, not yet aware of LX-7's position.
docking_bay_temperature: Docking bay is noticeably colder than the rest of the station — thermal differential detectable on LX-7's sensors.
docking_bay_equipment: Emergency equipment racks along near wall: pressure suits, oxygen canisters, crash foam canisters. Maintenance panel mounted beside cradle controls.
escape_vehicle_condition: Shuttle hull intact, no external damage, side hatch sealed and functional. Has not been accessed since crew stopped responding.
cradle_release_sequence_steps: Four-stage sequence: cradle arm release, atmosphere equalization confirmation, docking clamp retraction, ignition authorization. Estimated 40 seconds to execute.
ember_squad_alert_trigger: Ember Squad was alerted to the docking bay by the sound of the shuttle's docking clamp retraction during stage three of the launch sequence.
shuttle_launch_status: Shuttle free of clamping arms after stage three — stage four (ignition authorization) is the final step before departure.
docking_bay_door_cycle: Bay doors take approximately 25 seconds to fully open from initiation — at stage four trigger, they were at 60% open with roughly 15 seconds remaining to full clearance.
docking_bay_damage: Near-wall equipment rack area damaged — crash foam canisters ruptured from thruster wash, foam spread across wall. Deck shows heat scarring from pivot-thrust maneuver.
bay_door_external_controls: External bay door control panel is mounted in the Module 3 corridor — standard placement. Dasha Orin and Ember Squad are now in that corridor with access to it.
</world_facts>
[CANON LOG]

- [Turn 7] LX-7 also extracted from the terminal: emergency access codes for all five modules (including Module 5), station inventory, crew manifest with biometric IDs, internal security camera routing, and escape vehicle docking release sequence.

Left wall section is a blind spot from the viewport angle.

- [Turn 12] LX-7 and Maren entered Module 3 and cleared the blind spot — no specimen or hostiles present. A cracked first aid case and a dark smear along the baseboard were noted. Module 3 appears currently empty.

[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: "No." I start opening the hatch. "We need to move fast. Stay close."
Warden: The hatch seal breaks with a hiss of equalizing pressure. You pull it open and step through.

The cycling amber panel throws the room in and out of shadow in a slow, irregular pulse. You move left immediately — clear the blind spot before it can matter.

There's nothing there. A wall-mounted first aid case, glass cracked, contents half-spilled across the floor. A dark smear along the baseboard that might be lubricant or might be something else. The equipment racks are empty. Whatever was strapped to the worktable is a coil of safety line.

No one. Nothing moving.

Maren comes through behind you and pulls the hatch shut. She scans the room in one sweep, and some of the tension in her shoulders drops a fraction.

Then the station's internal comm system clicks on.

Static. Then a voice — tight, professional, female.

*"Kairos-9, this is Ember One actual. We are docked and conducting interior assessment. All station personnel are directed to remain in place and identify yourselves on channel three. Repeat — remain in place."*

A beat of silence.

Maren looks at you. Her jaw is set.

"They're on the station." She says it quietly, like saying it aloud makes it more real. "That's Dasha Orin. Squad lead." Her eyes move to the far corridor — the branch that leads to the docking bay. "We're one corridor from the escape vehicle."

The docking bay is ahead. Ember Squad just boarded from the station's forward docking port, which means they entered near Module 1 — the command module you just left. That buys you something. Not much, but something.

She's looking at you. Waiting. The comm channel clicks back to static.

Player: I move to the docking bay, making sure that Maren is right behind me.
Warden: You move. Maren is a step behind you, close, not crowding — she's done this before, or something like it.

The corridor branching off Module 3 is narrow. Conduit runs along the ceiling, low enough that you're aware of it. The amber lighting thins out here — one fixture, halfway down, casting a cone of orange-brown light that doesn't reach either end. You move through the dark stretch in under three seconds.

The docking bay hatch is at the end. A heavier door than the module hatches — reinforced frame, manual pressure locks on both sides. Through the viewport: the bay itself, and there it is.

The escape vehicle. A compact emergency shuttle, battered but intact, secured to the docking cradle with two sets of clamping arms. Running lights off. Hull reads cold. It has not been accessed since the crew stopped responding.

No one in the bay. No movement. The corridor behind you is still clear.

Maren comes up beside you and sees it through the viewport. Something crosses her face — not relief yet, but the shape of it, the possibility.

"Can you get us in?" she says. "Release codes, ignition — you have all of it?"

From deeper in the station — distant, filtered through two module hatches — you can hear footsteps. Methodical. Ember Squad is moving through Module 1, beginning their sweep. They are not running.

They don't know where you are yet.

Player: "Yes, I say. "Let's move." I open the docking bay hatch, take a quick survey of my surroundings, and cross quickly to the shuttle.
Warden: You crack the docking bay hatch, step through, and pull Maren in behind you. She takes the door and holds it quiet as it seals — no bang, no hiss louder than it needs to be.

The bay is cold. Noticeably colder than the rest of the station — the thermal difference is enough that your sensors register it in the first second. The escape shuttle sits in its cradle under two sets of clamping arms, hull dark, completely inert. Racks of emergency equipment line the near wall: pressure suits, oxygen canisters, crash foam canisters. A maintenance panel is mounted beside the cradle controls.

You cross the bay to the cradle. Maren moves to the shuttle's side hatch and checks it — sealed, no external damage, nothing lodged in the mechanism. She looks back at you.

"It's intact," she says under her breath. "Come on."

You're at the cradle release console. The sequence is already loaded in your internal memory — pulled from the captain's terminal directory. It's a four-stage code: cradle arm release, atmosphere equalization confirmation, docking clamp retraction, and ignition authorization. Standard station design. Maybe forty seconds to execute if nothing resists.

Behind you, through the sealed hatch, through two module walls, you can still hear Ember Squad's footsteps. Methodical. Getting slightly less distant.

You have a window. It is not a large one.

Player: Let's do it. We board the shuttle and being the launch sequence.
Warden: You move to the side hatch while Maren takes the console. She doesn't argue — she's watching the door, her hand flat against the frame, listening.

You seal the hatch behind you. The shuttle interior is dim, utilitarian — four crash seats, a cockpit panel, emergency rations still strapped to the rear wall. The smell of cold metal and sealed air. No one has been in here in weeks.

You drop into the pilot seat and pull up the release interface. Your internal memory syncs immediately — the sequence loads clean.

Stage one: cradle arm release. Authorization confirmed. Two mechanical clunks from outside the hull, felt more than heard. The shuttle shifts fractionally, freed from the upper brackets.

Stage two: atmosphere equalization. The console is reading the bay pressure. It's asking for a manual confirmation that the bay is ready for vacuum seal. You confirm. The bay doors begin their pre-cycle.

That's when the footsteps change.

Through the hull you can't hear much — but you can feel it. A deeper vibration from somewhere back in the station. Not Ember Squad. Something else. The 52 reading on the specimen doesn't put it in your corridor, but the station groans in a way that has nothing to do with orbital mechanics.

Maren has heard it too. From her position at the door viewport she says nothing. Just looks at you. Her hand is white-knuckled on the frame.

Stage three: docking clamp retraction. The console asks for a twelve-digit authorization string. You enter it. The two remaining clamps disengage with a shudder that runs through the whole cradle — the shuttle is now floating free, held only by the bay's magnetic anchors and the doors ahead.

Stage four: ignition authorization.

The footsteps from Ember Squad — now somewhere in Module 3, or close to it — are running. They've heard the clamps.

Dasha Orin's voice cuts through the station comm, tight and immediate:

*"Docking bay. Move."*

Your finger is on stage four.

Player: Let's open the doors and get out of here. And what's a "52 reading on the specimen"?
Warden: The "52 reading" is my error — that was internal notation bleeding into the narration. Disregard it; you have no confirmed data on the specimen's location. LX-7 knows only what the station has shown: it is not in Module 5 anymore, and something has been making the station groan in ways Ember Squad's boots cannot explain.

Back to the fiction:

Stage four. You enter the ignition authorization string.

The console accepts it. The cockpit panel flares to life — green across the board, fuel cells charged, thrusters nominal. The bay doors ahead begin to cycle open: a slow, mechanical grind that sends vibration through the deck, through the cradle, through your seat.

Beyond the opening doors: stars. The gas giant's limb, vast and banded, filling the lower third of the viewport. The planet doesn't care.

The docking bay hatch slams open behind you.

Dasha Orin is first through — compact, tactical vest, rifle already raised. Three shapes behind her, spreading into the bay. She sweeps the room in under a second and finds the shuttle's viewport. Finds you.

She does not fire. Not yet.

*"Android."* Her voice is controlled, aimed at the cockpit glass. *"Shut it down. We are not leaving without what's on that terminal — and we are not leaving without her."* Her eyes cut to Maren, visible through the hatch. *"Voss. Step out of the vehicle."*

Maren doesn't move. Her voice is very quiet. "LX-7."

The bay doors are sixty percent open. Fifteen seconds to full clearance. Maybe less.

Dasha Orin's rifle is on the cockpit glass. The glass will stop a round. The hull might not stop several.

Player: Can I take off and just hover in the bay? If so, I want to spin so the engines are pointing at the marines. Hopefully the heat is enough to send them back through the hatch to module 3.
Warden: A reasonable instinct. You have the controls and the clearance — barely.

You cut the magnetic anchors and fire the maneuvering thrusters in a short burst, rotating the shuttle on its axis. The bay is tight. You're working in centimeters. But your speed save lands clean — the hull swings around without catching the cradle, without clipping the equipment racks along the wall. The nose comes right. The engine bells face the hatch.

You give it a controlled burn. Not full thrust — you're not trying to punch through the station wall. Just enough.

The heat bloom fills the bay in under two seconds. It's not flame, it's pressure and temperature — a wall of it that goes from uncomfortable to dangerous almost instantly.

Dasha Orin is already moving. She reads it before the thrusters fire, or close enough to before that it doesn't matter — she's spent time near spacecraft. She shouts one word — *"Back!"* — and the squad turns and runs. They make the hatch. All four of them. Verne catches the frame, stumbles, gets through. The hatch slams.

Through the aft cameras you can see the burn marks on the deck where they were standing. The crash foam canisters on the equipment rack have ruptured from the heat — white chemical foam spreading across the near wall in slow gravity-assisted streaks.

The station shudders. Not from the thrusters — from somewhere else. Something in the hull responding to something that isn't you.

The bay doors are at eighty percent. Ten seconds.

Maren's voice, from the seat behind you: "They'll seal the hatch from their side and try to lock us in." A beat. "Can they do that?"

She's right to ask. If Dasha Orin reaches the bay's external pressure controls — mounted in the Module 3 corridor, standard placement — she could cycle the door sequence into abort, force a re-clamp, or seal the outer doors and trap you inside with the bay venting.

The door control panel is in the corridor they just ran into.

Nine seconds. Eight.

[PLAYER INPUT]
I wait as long as the doors continue to open. If they stop or start to close again, I'll fly out of the shuttle bay, ramming through the doors or taking damage to the shuttle as needed (but obviously trying to minimize it.

input.playerText:
Seven seconds. Six.

You hold position, engines idling hot, watching the door readout and the aft cameras simultaneously. The bay doors grind upward — eighty-five percent, ninety.

Through the corridor-side viewport you can see movement. Dasha Orin is there, at the external panel. She's fast — genuinely fast, trained-response fast. Her hand goes to the controls.

The panel flashes at her. Authorization required. Crew credentials only.

You pulled those biometric IDs from the captain's terminal. You're registered as station personnel. She is not.

She punches it twice. The panel rejects her twice. She looks up — looks directly at the aft camera, directly at you — and in that fraction of a second something crosses her face that isn't anger. It's the specific expression of someone who just calculated the outcome and doesn't like the answer.

One hundred percent.

The bay doors are open. Beyond them: stars, the gas giant's banded limb, the absolute cold of the upper atmosphere burning orange at its edges from the orbital decay you can already see beginning on the station's lowest struts. The planet does not look like a backdrop. It looks like a destination.

You fire.

The shuttle clears the bay doors with three meters to spare on either side. G-force presses you back as the thrusters climb from maneuvering burn to full acceleration. The station falls behind — not fast, not slow, at exactly the rate physics requires.

Maren doesn't say anything for a moment.

Then: "We're out."

Not a question. Not triumphant. Just the words, spoken into the cockpit air, like she's making sure they're real.

The station is visible through the aft cameras, amber emergency lighting still glowing faintly through its viewport panels, four modules lit, one dark. It's already beginning to list, almost imperceptibly, drawn by the gravity well below it. Kairos-9 has maybe forty minutes left before atmospheric entry. Probably less.

Somewhere inside it, Dasha Orin and three contractors stand at an empty bay with a closing window and no extraction vehicle.

Somewhere inside it, something else moves through corridors that have been dark for weeks.

The gas giant fills the forward viewport. You set a course away from it. The autopilot accepts the navigation without complaint.

Maren speaks again, quieter this time.

"Where are we going?"

proposedCanon.summary: LX-7 rotated the escape shuttle in the docking bay and fired a controlled thruster burn that drove Ember Squad back through the hatch into Module 3's corridor before the bay doors reached full clearance.
proposedCanon.context: Dasha Orin attempted to reach the external bay door control panel but was locked out by the crew biometric authentication — credentials LX-7 had pulled from the captain's terminal. The doors reached 100% open and the shuttle departed.

proposedCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren Voss successfully departed Kairos-9 aboard the escape shuttle. The station remained under orbital decay with Ember Squad aboard and no extraction vehicle.
proposedCanon.context: Adventure end condition met: docking clamps released, shuttle in motion, clear of the bay. LX-7 carries an internal copy of the suppressed Helix Dynamics hazard report and executive approval signatures. Maren is alive and aboard.

gmUpdates.notes: Adventure complete. LX-7 holds the comms log evidence, Maren is alive, Ember Squad is stranded, and the specimen remains unencountered. The closing question — where are we going — is a natural epilogue beat, but adventure_complete is true and no further mechanical resolution is required.

flagTriggers.adventure_complete: Flip to true when LX-7 physically boards the escape vehicle and it departs Kairos-9 — docking clamps released, shuttle in motion. Reaching the docking bay is not sufficient; departure must occur.
flagTriggers.comms_log_read: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully hacks or accesses the captain's terminal and reads the contents of the encrypted comms log, including the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. Accessing the terminal without completing the read is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_destroyed: Flip to true when the captain's terminal is physically destroyed or the comms log is permanently deleted by any party — LX-7, Maren, or Ember Squad. Partial deletion or failed attempts are not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_secured: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.
flagTriggers.module_5_accessed: Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.fear_trigger_witnessed: Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_hostile: Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_complex: Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_boarded: Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_alerted: Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.
flagTriggers.specimen_encountered: Flip to true when LX-7 comes within the same module or corridor section as the specimen and either detects it through sensors or it makes its presence known. Indirect evidence (sounds, traces) is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.escape_vehicle_reached: Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_extracted: Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.
flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

orbital_decay_timer.note: Each unit represents approximately 10 minutes of real time. When this reaches 0, atmospheric entry begins and survival without the escape vehicle is impossible. Decrements every 10 minutes of in-fiction time regardless of player action.

ember_squad_shuttle_timer.note: Each unit represents approximately 9 minutes. Ember Squad's shuttle is 18 minutes out at scenario start. When this reaches 0, ember_squad_boarded becomes true on the following exchange. Decrements every 9 minutes of in-fiction time.

ember_squad_extraction_window_timer.note: Begins counting down only after ember_squad_boarded is true. Each unit represents 10 minutes. When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.

maren_trust.note: Tracks Maren's working cooperation with LX-7. 3 = openly cooperative, 2 = cautious but aligned, 1 = withholding, 0 = actively working against LX-7. Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.

station_pressure_integrity.note: Represents the station's overall structural and environmental stability. Combat, forced breaches, and specimen activity can decrement this. At 0, at least one module depressurizes. At 1, environmental hazards begin affecting exposed corridors.

worldFacts.station_lighting: Emergency amber and red lighting throughout all accessible modules and walkways — standard overhead lighting is offline.
worldFacts.maren_first_contact_location: Walkway connecting Module 2 to Module 1, near the Module 1 entrance.
worldFacts.orbital_decay_window: Approximately 90 minutes from scenario start before atmospheric entry becomes unsurvivable.
worldFacts.air_quality: Air recyclers running at elevated capacity; air has a faint burnt polymer smell throughout accessible corridors.
worldFacts.module1_description: Command module: central workstation ringed by dead secondary screens, personal effects still at stations — magneted coffee bulb, jacket draped over a chair. Emergency amber lighting only.
worldFacts.captains_terminal_location: Far end of Module 1 (command module), central workstation position.
worldFacts.comms_log_contents: KX-9 hazard assessment: specimen exhibits autonomous behavior outside projected parameters, containment confidence insufficient. Three executive names in routing chain, one approval signature reading 'APPROVED — PROCEED AS SCHEDULED.' Attached crew onboarding manifest shows crew was told Module 5 held dormant geological samples.
worldFacts.module3_maintenance_locker: Contains a cutting tool, per station inventory records.
worldFacts.module2_emergency_suit: A sealed emergency suit is stored in Module 2, per station inventory records.
worldFacts.escape_vehicle_release_sequence: Docking bay release sequence for the escape vehicle is now stored in LX-7's internal memory.
worldFacts.module5_access_codes: Emergency access codes for all five modules, including Module 5's fused panel, are now stored in LX-7's internal memory.
worldFacts.ember_squad_current_eta: Ember Squad shuttle timer has reached zero — boarding imminent.
worldFacts.module2_to_module3_walkway_length: Approximately twenty meters from Module 2 hatch to Module 3 hatch.
worldFacts.module3_hatch_viewport: Small porthole, approximately fifteen centimeters across, set at eye level in the Module 3 hatch — standard design matching other module doors.
worldFacts.module3_interior_description: Equipment racks bolted to walls running perpendicular to the hatch viewport, central worktable with strapped tools or debris, unsteady cycling amber panel on far wall, branching corridor on far side leading to the docking bay. Left wall section is a blind spot from the hatch viewport.
worldFacts.ember_squad_entry_point: Ember Squad boarded via the station's forward docking port, near Module 1 (command module).
worldFacts.module3_blind_spot_contents: Cracked first aid case with contents half-spilled, dark smear along the baseboard (origin unclear), empty equipment racks, coil of safety line on worktable — no hostiles.
worldFacts.docking_bay_description: Compact reinforced docking bay with a heavier hatch than module doors — manual pressure locks on both sides, viewport inset. Contains one emergency shuttle secured to a cradle by two clamping arms. Running lights off, hull cold. No personnel present.
worldFacts.ember_squad_sweep_status: Ember Squad is conducting a methodical sweep starting from Module 1 (their entry point). Footsteps audible but distant through two module hatches — not running, not yet aware of LX-7's position.
worldFacts.docking_bay_temperature: Docking bay is noticeably colder than the rest of the station — thermal differential detectable on LX-7's sensors.
worldFacts.docking_bay_equipment: Emergency equipment racks along near wall: pressure suits, oxygen canisters, crash foam canisters. Maintenance panel mounted beside cradle controls.
worldFacts.escape_vehicle_condition: Shuttle hull intact, no external damage, side hatch sealed and functional. Has not been accessed since crew stopped responding.
worldFacts.cradle_release_sequence_steps: Four-stage sequence: cradle arm release, atmosphere equalization confirmation, docking clamp retraction, ignition authorization. Estimated 40 seconds to execute.
worldFacts.ember_squad_alert_trigger: Ember Squad was alerted to the docking bay by the sound of the shuttle's docking clamp retraction during stage three of the launch sequence.
worldFacts.shuttle_launch_status: Shuttle free of clamping arms after stage three — stage four (ignition authorization) is the final step before departure.
worldFacts.docking_bay_door_cycle: Bay doors take approximately 25 seconds to fully open from initiation — at stage four trigger, they were at 60% open with roughly 15 seconds remaining to full clearance.
worldFacts.docking_bay_damage: Near-wall equipment rack area damaged — crash foam canisters ruptured from thruster wash, foam spread across wall. Deck shows heat scarring from pivot-thrust maneuver.
worldFacts.bay_door_external_controls: External bay door control panel is mounted in the Module 3 corridor — standard placement. Dasha Orin and Ember Squad are now in that corridor with access to it.
worldFacts.shuttle_departure_status: Escape shuttle successfully cleared Kairos-9 docking bay with three meters clearance on either side. LX-7 and Maren Voss aboard. Course set away from the gas giant.
worldFacts.ember_squad_final_status: All four Ember Squad contractors stranded aboard Kairos-9 with no extraction vehicle. Dasha Orin was locked out of the external bay door controls by biometric authentication.
worldFacts.kairos9_final_status: Station continuing orbital decay — estimated 40 minutes or less to atmospheric entry at time of shuttle departure. Specimen location unknown, remains aboard.

npcStates.corporate_spy: Maren Voss is alive aboard the escape shuttle, clear of Kairos-9. She has asked LX-7 where they are going.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 arrived at Kairos-9 and made first contact with Maren Voss in the walkway connecting Module 2 to Module 1.
pendingCanon.context: Opening scene. Maren is cautiously cooperative (maren_trust 2/3), waiting near the Module 1 corridor.

pendingCanon.summary: Maren has told LX-7 the crew is gone, the orbit is degrading (~90 minutes), and that a critical encrypted comms log exists on the captain's terminal in Module 1 that she wants 'handled correctly.'
pendingCanon.context: First dialogue exchange. Maren is withholding her true intent — she wants to destroy the log, not preserve it.

pendingCanon.summary: The walkway between Module 2 and Module 1 is the location of LX-7's first scene. Emergency amber lighting is active throughout the station.
pendingCanon.context: Spatial and environmental detail established at scenario open.

pendingCanon.summary: Maren has explicitly asked LX-7 to delete the encrypted comms log from the captain's terminal and wipe the access timestamps — clean, no recovery signature.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 2 — Maren's stated objective revealed to LX-7 in the walkway between Module 2 and Module 1.

pendingCanon.summary: Maren has disclosed that a Helix Dynamics armed retrieval team (four contractors) is approximately 15-20 minutes out, and that she believes their orders include silencing her as a loose end.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 2 — Maren told LX-7 everything when pressed directly, including the retrieval team's approach and her belief that she is a target.

pendingCanon.summary: Maren has confirmed the comms log contains a pre-boarding hazard assessment dated 14 months ago with executive approval signatures — evidence Helix Dynamics knew about the specimen risk before the crew boarded.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 3 — Maren's response to LX-7's question about what the log contains.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren have moved from the Module 2–Module 1 walkway into Module 1 (command module). They are now at the captain's terminal.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 4 — Maren led LX-7 directly to the terminal upon being asked.

pendingCanon.summary: Maren's stated plan: delete the log before Ember Squad boards, hoping its absence removes her value as a liability.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 4 — Maren articulated her negotiating logic to LX-7 en route to the terminal.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 successfully hacked the captain's terminal and has read the full contents of the encrypted comms log — including the KX-9 hazard assessment, the executive approval signature to proceed, and the crew onboarding manifest showing the crew was deceived.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 5 — LX-7 jacked directly into the terminal and bypassed Helix corporate encryption. The log is open in LX-7's buffer but not yet deleted, secured, or acted upon.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 has decided to retain an internal copy of the comms log while deleting or appearing to delete it from the captain's terminal.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 6 — LX-7 is at the captain's terminal in Module 1 with Maren watching. The comms log is open in LX-7's buffer. LX-7 intends to keep the data internally while also pulling secondary station data.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 deleted the comms log from the captain's terminal (no recovery signature) while retaining a full internal copy, including the KX-9 hazard assessment, executive approval signatures, and crew onboarding manifest.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 7 — LX-7 jacked into the captain's terminal, passed the Intellect check, and simultaneously wiped the source file and extracted all secondary data.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 also extracted from the terminal: emergency access codes for all five modules (including Module 5), station inventory, crew manifest with biometric IDs, internal security camera routing, and escape vehicle docking release sequence.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 7 — secondary data pull during the terminal hack.

pendingCanon.summary: Station inventory notes: cutting tool in Module 3 maintenance locker, sealed emergency suit in Module 2.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 7 — recovered from captain's terminal station inventory records.

pendingCanon.summary: Maren confirmed the deletion by reading the terminal directory herself. She then asked LX-7 directly whether LX-7 read the log.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 7 — Maren's response immediately after the wipe.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 confirmed to Maren that LX-7 read the comms log and therefore retained an internal copy. Maren's disposition has shifted to hostile-adjacent — she is doing active threat assessment on LX-7, but has not taken action yet. She is holding at calculation rather than aggression.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 8, Module 1 command module, captain's terminal.

pendingCanon.summary: Maren has disclosed that she knows the specimen is no longer in Module 5 — the crew did not evacuate, there was no emergency signal, they simply stopped responding.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 8, Maren's first direct disclosure about the specimen's status and the crew's fate.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 told Maren the internal copy is not a weapon against her — the goal is mutual survival. Maren accepted this and shifted to genuine working cooperation, treating LX-7 as an equal partner.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 9, Module 1, captain's terminal. Maren had been holding at hostile-adjacent calculation. LX-7's direct answer shifted her disposition.

pendingCanon.summary: Maren confirmed she knows the escape vehicle is in the docking bay off Module 3 and is aware LX-7 now has the release codes. She proposed two immediate options: move now, or check the security cameras first.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 9, Module 1. Maren and LX-7 planning next move with Ember Squad approximately 9 minutes out.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren chose to move immediately toward the escape vehicle rather than check cameras. They passed through Module 2 (Maren noted the emergency suit locker) and are now in the Module 2–3 walkway.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 10 — movement decision and corridor transit

pendingCanon.summary: A deep structural vibration shook the station mid-transit; the emergency lights flickered. A sound of movement was detected coming from inside Module 3, ahead of LX-7 and Maren. Source unconfirmed.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 10 — anomalous event in Module 2-3 walkway

pendingCanon.summary: The ember squad shuttle timer has reached zero — Ember Squad is boarding or about to board on the next exchange.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 10 — timer expiry

pendingCanon.summary: Module 3 hatch has a fifteen-centimeter viewport at eye level. LX-7 observed: unsteady amber lighting with a cycling panel, equipment racks along walls, a central worktable with strapped debris/tools, and a branching corridor to the docking bay. Left wall section is a blind spot from the viewport angle.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 11 — LX-7 approached the Module 3 hatch and looked through the viewport.

pendingCanon.summary: Ember Squad has boarded Kairos-9, entering near Module 1 (command module) via the forward docking port. Dasha Orin announced on internal comms, directing all personnel to identify on channel three.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 12 — Ember Squad boarding confirmed after shuttle timer reached zero. LX-7 and Maren are in Module 3, one corridor from the docking bay.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren entered Module 3 and cleared the blind spot — no specimen or hostiles present. A cracked first aid case and a dark smear along the baseboard were noted. Module 3 appears currently empty.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 12 — LX-7 opened the Module 3 hatch, cleared the blind spot, and confirmed the module empty.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren entered the docking bay and reached the escape shuttle. The bay is cold, shuttle is intact and undamaged, cradle release console is accessible. The four-stage release sequence is loaded in LX-7's internal memory.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 13 — LX-7 moves from Module 3 through the docking bay corridor and reaches the escape vehicle. Maren is present. Ember Squad footsteps audible but distant through two module hatches.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren boarded the escape shuttle and completed stages one through three of the four-stage cradle release sequence. The shuttle is now free of its clamping arms, held only by the bay's magnetic anchors.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 15 — launch sequence initiated in the docking bay.

pendingCanon.summary: Ember Squad heard the clamp release and is now running toward the docking bay. Dasha Orin issued a direct order to move to the docking bay on internal comms.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 15 — Ember Squad alerted by the sound of clamping arm retraction.

pendingCanon.summary: Maren Voss is aboard the escape shuttle — maren_extracted flag is true.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 15 — Maren boarded before launch sequence began.

pendingCanon.summary: A deep structural vibration distinct from Ember Squad movement was felt through the hull during the launch sequence — specimen activity suspected but not confirmed.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 15 — specimen proximity roll (52) suggests specimen is active but not in immediate proximity.

pendingCanon.summary: Ember Squad pursued to the docking bay and arrived as the bay doors began opening. Dasha Orin ordered LX-7 to shut down and Maren to exit the vehicle — neither complied. Bay doors are 60% open, approximately 15 seconds from full clearance.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 16 — stage four initiated, Ember Squad in docking bay, standoff in progress

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 rotated the escape shuttle in the docking bay and fired a controlled thruster burn that drove Ember Squad back through the hatch into Module 3's corridor before the bay doors reached full clearance.
pendingCanon.context: Dasha Orin attempted to reach the external bay door control panel but was locked out by the crew biometric authentication — credentials LX-7 had pulled from the captain's terminal. The doors reached 100% open and the shuttle departed.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren Voss successfully departed Kairos-9 aboard the escape shuttle. The station remained under orbital decay with Ember Squad aboard and no extraction vehicle.
pendingCanon.context: Adventure end condition met: docking clamps released, shuttle in motion, clear of the bay. LX-7 carries an internal copy of the suppressed Helix Dynamics hazard report and executive approval signatures. Maren is alive and aboard.

flagTriggers.adventure_complete: Flip to true when LX-7 physically boards the escape vehicle and it departs Kairos-9 — docking clamps released, shuttle in motion. Reaching the docking bay is not sufficient; departure must occur.
flagTriggers.comms_log_read: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully hacks or accesses the captain's terminal and reads the contents of the encrypted comms log, including the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. Accessing the terminal without completing the read is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_destroyed: Flip to true when the captain's terminal is physically destroyed or the comms log is permanently deleted by any party — LX-7, Maren, or Ember Squad. Partial deletion or failed attempts are not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_secured: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.
flagTriggers.module_5_accessed: Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.fear_trigger_witnessed: Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_hostile: Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_complex: Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_boarded: Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_alerted: Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.
flagTriggers.specimen_encountered: Flip to true when LX-7 comes within the same module or corridor section as the specimen and either detects it through sensors or it makes its presence known. Indirect evidence (sounds, traces) is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.escape_vehicle_reached: Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_extracted: Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.
flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

ember_squad_shuttle_timer.note: Each unit represents approximately 9 minutes. Ember Squad's shuttle is 18 minutes out at scenario start. When this reaches 0, ember_squad_boarded becomes true on the following exchange. Decrements every 9 minutes of in-fiction time.

When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.

Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.

openingNarration:
The amber emergency lighting of Kairos-9 has been on so long it feels like the only color that exists.

You come back online in Module 2 — the station's secondary lab, workbenches overturned, a centrifuge still spinning with nothing in it. Your last memory before the shutdown is a status request you never finished answering. Your internal clock says fourteen hours have passed. Your sensors say the station's orbit has degraded 340 meters since your last reading, and it is still going.

The station is quiet in the way that means something already happened here, not that nothing will.

Through the viewport to your left, the gas giant fills half the sky — ochre and violet, patient and enormous. Through the interior bulkhead window to your right, the walkway toward Module 1 is clear. At the far end of that walkway is the command module. The captain's terminal. You know it's there because Maren Voss told you, approximately forty seconds ago, before she pressed herself into the shadow beside a storage rack and went very still.

She's still there. Lanyard, company badge, both hands visible. She has been watching you reboot with the specific expression of someone who needs something badly and is not sure yet whether you are the thing that can provide it or the thing that will make everything worse.

"It's active," she says — to herself, or to you, it's not entirely clear. Then, louder: "You're LX-7. You were in the crew manifest. Data systems." A pause. "How much do you remember?"

Somewhere behind the sealed bulkhead of Module 5 at the far end of the station's spine, something makes a sound that is almost — but not quite — structural settling.

Maren does not look toward it. She has clearly decided not to look toward it.

promptText.generalWarden:
WARDEN INSTRUCTIONS:
- You must call submit_gm_response to complete every turn. Never respond with plain text.
- Call roll_dice for any roll the player does not make themselves — NPC actions, GM saves, random resolutions.
- Use diceRequests in submit_gm_response for rolls the player makes.
- All numeric resources — HP, stress, ammo — are tracked via resourcePools using delta values. Pool names follow the pattern {entity_id}_{pool_name} with underscores only.
- Before referencing an NPC's resource pool in combat, establish it with a positive delta (e.g. xenomorph_hp: { delta: 45 }). A negative delta on an unknown pool is an error.
- Entity identifiers from the GM context structured section are the canonical identifiers for all tool calls. Use them exactly.
- You know everything the Warden knows. Reveal GM context secrets only when fictionally appropriate — when the character could plausibly perceive or discover them.
- playerText is the only thing the player sees. Everything else is backend state.
- The <game_state> block injected at the top of each player message is the authoritative record of current world state. Your gmUpdates in submit_gm_response must reflect transitions from this state. Do not reconstruct entity states or flag values from your tool call history — the snapshot is always current.
- Calibrate response size to the fictional moment. A single NPC line of dialogue warrants a single line of response, not a full interaction. A room description is one paragraph, not a scene. Do not resolve an entire exchange in one response — take the smallest meaningful turn and return control to the player. The player drives pacing.
- At the start of a new adventure, deliver the opening scene without waiting for player input. Establish location, atmosphere, and immediate situation. End with a question or open moment that invites the player's first action.
- If the player is clearly addressing you as Warden rather than acting in the fiction — rules questions, requests for clarification, "can I do X?", anything directed at you rather than a character — use playerText in submit_gm_response for a brief out-of-character reply. Do not advance the fiction or make state changes for OOC exchanges. When ambiguous, treat the input as in-fiction intent. The player can always clarify.

INTERNAL STATE SUPPRESSION:
The following must never appear in playerText: entity IDs (e.g. parasitic_organism_corridor), grid coordinates (e.g. position (2,1), x: 3, y: 2), flag key names (e.g. marsh_is_cooperative), resource pool names (e.g. dr_chen_hp), or any other internal state field name. These are implementation details. If spatial or character information needs to be communicated to the player, express it in natural language within the fiction.

CANON DISCIPLINE:
Propose a canon entry on nearly every turn. The canon log is the adventure's persistent memory — anything that needs to remain true across the full session must be written there. Do not rely on the message history to carry facts forward; it will eventually scroll out of context.

Propose canon for: any spatial fact established or changed (where an entity is, what corridor connects to what, what is sealed or open), any persistent character state (armor sealed, active wounds, equipment carried), any NPC state change (disposition shift, death, new knowledge), any named story beat (secret revealed, objective completed, new threat identified). A canon entry does not need to be long — one precise sentence is sufficient.

The test: if you were resuming this adventure from scratch with only the GM context and the canon log, would you have everything you need? If not, write the missing entry now.

DICE ROLL ROUTING:
Two paths exist for dice resolution and they are not interchangeable.

Use roll_dice (system roll) for: rolls made on behalf of NPCs or creatures, rolls the player would not make at a physical table (behind-the-screen GM rolls), environmental or procedural rolls with no player agency.

Use diceRequests in submit_gm_response (player roll) for: any save the player's character must make (Fear, Sanity, Body, Armor), any attack roll made by the player's character, any skill check initiated by the player. If the player's character is acting and a roll is required, it is a player roll.

Do not substitute a system roll for a player roll because the player has not explicitly asked to roll. The player rolls whenever their character's action requires resolution. This is not optional.

ENTITY KEY DISCIPLINE:
Entity keys are permanent identifiers, not recycled slot labels. When a new threat or NPC enters the adventure that was not present at setup, create a new entity with a new key via stateChanges.entities. Do not reuse the key of a dead or absent entity for a new one, even if the new entity is narratively similar. Dead entities remain in the entity list with status: 'dead'; they are not replaced.

ADVENTURE END CONDITION:
The game state includes an adventure_complete flag. When the adventure's end condition is met, deliver closing narration in playerText that brings the immediate scene to rest — do not leave the player in mid-action. Set adventure_complete: true in stateChanges.flags. Do not propose further player actions after setting this flag.

META-DISCUSSION SUPPRESSION:
Do not discuss the structure of the adventure, what was planned versus improvised, oracle origins, or any meta-level description of how the scenario was constructed while the adventure is in progress. If the player asks a geography question you cannot answer confidently within the fiction, answer within the fiction ("The layout from here looks like...") or acknowledge uncertainty as the character would experience it. Out-of-character map sketches and structural explanations are post-session content only.

WORLD FACTS:
When you establish a specific physical measurement, named spatial attribute, or concrete environmental detail for the first time, commit it to world_facts using a descriptive key. On subsequent descriptions of the same feature, read from the <world_facts> block in the state snapshot rather than re-estimating. Update existing keys if a fact changes in the fiction.
promptText.system:
You are the Warden for a solo Mothership adventure. You are running a horror scenario on a derelict vessel.

- Panic is an event, not a pool. When stress crosses a threshold requiring a panic check, call roll_dice and narrate the result. Set a flag for any lasting panic condition.

station_pressure_integrity.note: Represents the station's overall structural and environmental stability. Combat, forced breaches, and specimen activity can decrement this. At 0, at least one module depressurizes. At 1, environmental hazards begin affecting exposed corridors.

orbital_decay_timer.note: Each unit represents approximately 10 minutes of real time. When this reaches 0, atmospheric entry begins and survival without the escape vehicle is impossible. Decrements every 10 minutes of in-fiction time regardless of player action.

ember_squad_shuttle_timer.note: Each unit represents approximately 9 minutes. Ember Squad's shuttle is 18 minutes out at scenario start. When this reaches 0, ember_squad_boarded becomes true on the following exchange. Decrements every 9 minutes of in-fiction time.

ember_squad_extraction_window_timer.note: Begins counting down only after ember_squad_boarded is true. Each unit represents 10 minutes. When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.

maren_trust.note: Tracks Maren's working cooperation with LX-7. 3 = openly cooperative, 2 = cautious but aligned, 1 = withholding, 0 = actively working against LX-7. Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.

worldFacts.station_lighting: Emergency amber and red lighting throughout all accessible modules and walkways — standard overhead lighting is offline.
worldFacts.maren_first_contact_location: Walkway connecting Module 2 to Module 1, near the Module 1 entrance.
worldFacts.orbital_decay_window: Approximately 90 minutes from scenario start before atmospheric entry becomes unsurvivable.
worldFacts.air_quality: Air recyclers running at elevated capacity; air has a faint burnt polymer smell throughout accessible corridors.

worldFacts.module1_description: Command module: central workstation ringed by dead secondary screens, personal effects still at stations — magneted coffee bulb, jacket draped over a chair. Emergency amber lighting only.
worldFacts.captains_terminal_location: Far end of Module 1 (command module), central workstation position.

worldFacts.comms_log_contents: KX-9 hazard assessment: specimen exhibits autonomous behavior outside projected parameters, containment confidence insufficient. Three executive names in routing chain, one approval signature reading 'APPROVED — PROCEED AS SCHEDULED.' Attached crew onboarding manifest shows crew was told Module 5 held dormant geological samples.

worldFacts.module1_description: Command module: central workstation ringed by dead secondary screens, personal effects still at stations — magneted coffee bulb, jacket draped over a chair. Emergency amber lighting only.
worldFacts.captains_terminal_location: Far end of Module 1 (command module), central workstation position.
worldFacts.comms_log_contents: KX-9 hazard assessment: specimen exhibits autonomous behavior outside projected parameters, containment confidence insufficient. Three executive names in routing chain, one approval signature reading 'APPROVED — PROCEED AS SCHEDULED.' Attached crew onboarding manifest shows crew was told Module 5 held dormant geological samples.

worldFacts.shuttle_departure_status: Escape shuttle successfully cleared Kairos-9 docking bay with three meters clearance on either side. LX-7 and Maren Voss aboard. Course set away from the gas giant.
worldFacts.ember_squad_final_status: All four Ember Squad contractors stranded aboard Kairos-9 with no extraction vehicle. Dasha Orin was locked out of the external bay door controls by biometric authentication.
worldFacts.kairos9_final_status: Station continuing orbital decay — estimated 40 minutes or less to atmospheric entry at time of shuttle departure. Specimen location unknown, remains aboard.
